ASEERGHUR, or Asseer, an important fortress of Hindostan, in the prov. of Candeish, 12 m. NE of Burhanpur. The surrounding territory belongs to Scindia; but A. is a British possession, and remarkable for its strength. The fortress crowns the top of a hill 750 ft. in alt. above the pettah or village of A. at its base, which in 1822 had a pop. of 2,000. The approach to it is across a wild tract of country so infested by tigers as to render it almost inaccessible.
ASEIAC, a town of Kuzistan, in Persia, 90 m. S of Ispahan.

ASELE, a parish and village of Swedish Lapland. The village is 85 m. W of Umeä, in N lat. 64° 13'. It has some trade in pottery, salted meat and fish, poultry, cheese, and butter.—The division of Asele- Lapmark is bounded on the N by the prov. of Umeä; on the E by the gulf of Bothnia; on the S by Angermannia and Jamtland; and on the W by Norway.

ASERRADORES, an island off the coast of Nicaragua, opposite the town of Realejo.

ASHANTEE, an extensive territory of Western Africa, lying immediately behind or to the N of the minor states which stretch along the Gold coast. It is of indefinite and varying political limits; but may be regarded as stretching on the N to the Kong mountains; and as separated from Dagomba and Dahomey on the E by the Rio Loka and the Rio Volta; on the S, the Ashantee authority has been shaken off, with British assistance, by the small maritime states of Aquapim, Fantee, Ahanta, and Amanahea; but the inland frontier of these territories is quite undefined. The river Assinee appears to form the W limit of the Ashantee territory from near its mouth a considerable way inland.—The country, which may comprise an area of 70,000 or 80,000 sq. m. is mountainous on the E and S; but towards the NW stretches away into an extensive plain. The geological formation is granite, quartz, and slate. The greater portion of the soil is argillaceous and aluminous. Ashantee is watered by the Tando and the Bara on the NW. These two streams unite to form the Assinee. To the E of this river is the Sinee or Ancobra; and to the E of the Ancobra is the Succondee, one branch of which, the Boosempra, comes from Coomassie, and joins the main stream on the r. bank. All these rivers have a general course from N to S. The Rio Volta, or Aswada, sweeps round the whole territory of A., skirting the base of the Kong mountains from W to E, and then turning SE to its junction with the Loka, after which its general course is SSE.—The rainy season commences in the latter end of May. From the end of October to April is the dry and hot season. At Coomassie in June, the therm. ranged from 73° to 84°; in October from 70° to 83°; in December from 63° to 85°; in January from 58° to 86°. A. from N lat. 7° 30' to the coast is described by M. Dupuis as "a solid rampart of vegetation," extending E and W from Aquapim to Ashantee. Lions are numerous on the northern frontiers; elephants abound in the Kong districts; baboons, hyenas, antelopes, stags, small horses, the rhinoceros, and hippopotamus, are enumerated among the animal species of this region. The sugar-cane, rice, the butter-tree, papaws, ananas, and bananas, are cultivated, but the yam appears to supply the chief article of subsistence to the natives. Manufactures and Commerce.] The Ashantees weave and dye cotton with considerable dexterity, and these stuffs form their staple articles of commerce; they also send gold-dust and vegetable butter to the Gold coast; and likewise trade with Dagomba, Fezzan, and the countries to the north. It is upon A. that the commerce of the coast almost exclusively rests; and the first notice of its importance in this respect was derived from the opposite extremity of Africa. Mr. Lucas, in 1792, heard it mentioned at Tripoli as the goal of a caravan-route of more than 2,000 m. across the broadest diagonal of the continent. The earlier accounts from the coast, indeed, describe the gold and ivory which form its staples, as drawn from Dinkra and other intermediate countries; but it is now ascertained that the bulk of these articles come from beyond even A., only passing through that kingdom. Palm-oil, an article of growing importance, is chiefly furnished from the immense forest extending from the frontier to Coomassie. The palm tree (Elais Guineensis) grows spontaneously throughout the whole of this part of Western Africa. The pop. of this kingdom certainly exceeds ASH 1,000,000; and this estimate applies only to the original Ashantee, not including its tributary nations, which are said to be 22 in number. The men are well-made, but less robust than the Fantees. Their physiognomy and laws reminded Bowdich of the ancient Egyptians; and, comparing this circumstance with their traditions, that traveller is of opinion that they are descended from a mixed body of Ethiopians and Egyptians who may have been driven from their primitive seats by some political convulsion. Their religion is a mixture of Islamism and Paganism. The diabolical custom of immolating human victims, as a part of the funeral rites of all persons of consequence, prevails. Bowdich tells us that the present king of Ashantee, a very "amiable and benevolent sovereign," on the death of his mother devoted 3,000 victims to "water her grave," 2,000 of whom were Fantee prisoners, and the rest were levied in certain proportions on the several towns! Suicide is frequent among the A., but is held in such abhorrence among the Fantees, that a family considers it one of the greatest misfortunes that can happen to it when one of its members is guilty of the crime. The Ashantees, high and low, neither read nor write. The only written language is the Arabic, and the only persons who can write it are Moors. This illiterate condition, however, seems not incompatible in their case with a considerable development of intelthe NE; and another, the Dah, rises to the E of lect and fancy. All the nations on this coast have a fluent and graceful oratory, with some talent at improvisatory poetry; and in these finer arts, the Ashantees display a superiority. Their habitations, though rude, are decidedly superior in cleanliness, neatness, and a certain degree of comfort, to those on the coast. Their cloths, woven of cotton mixed with silk, and some other ornamental articles, are fine and even elegant; though their manufacturing industry is on the whole inferior, not only to that of Europe, but of some countries on the Niger.—The Ashantee language is the softest and most harmonious dialect spoken on the Gold coast, abounding in vowels, and being destitute of aspirates. Like some of the dialects of North America, it is often highly figurative. Tone constitutes an essential part of this language, as it does in that of the Chinese: many words vary their signification according to the tone of the speaker. It has no articles, no inflections of nouns and pronouns, and no derivative adverbs. The languages of the Ashantees, Fantees, Assinese, and Aquapims are fundamentally one; but the Ashantee is considered by Mr. Bowdich as the Attic among the dialects of Western Africa. Government. The government, although monarchical and despotic in spirit, partakes considerably of aristocratical forms. A supreme council seems to conduct all political intercourse with foreigners. There is a great deal of pomp and parade about the court. On the occasion of Bowdich's mission, the councillors, caboceers, and captains appeared all seated under their umbrellas of scarlet or yellow cloth, silks, and cotton of every glaring variety, and decorated with carved and golden pelicans, panthers, baboons, crescents, &c. on the top; their shape generally being that of a dome. Distinct and pompous retinues stood around with gold elephant-tails to keep off the flies, gold-headed swords, embossed muskets, and other splendid novelties. Each chief was supported by the dignitaries of his own province, to his right and left; and it was truly concilium in concilio.-Coomassie, the capital of A., is a town of considerable size, situated in N lat. 6° 34′ 50", 200 leagues W from Benin. Bowdich estimates the pop. at 18,000 souls; and describes the palace as a magnificent edifice. History.] This kingdom,-the name of which, till very lately, had scarcely reached Europeans, —seems to be the most powerful, commercial, and civilized of any on the western coast of Africa. The Ashantees appear gradually to have obtained ascendancy over the surrounding states during the 18th cent. In the preceding cent. Dinkra was the paramount state in this quarter of Africa. Events of a disastrous nature brought this people under the recent notice of Europeans. In 1806, two chiefs who had been tributary to A. revolted; but, being totally defeated, sought refuge in the Fantee country. The king of A pursued them, but professed to have no hostile intentions against the Fantees. The latter, however, joined their forces to those of the fugitive chiefs, but found themselves to be no match for this new enemy; victorious in every encounter, the Ashantees took possession of Abrah, the capital of Fantee, and from this period they gradually extended their influence over the surrounding territories, until they at last came into collision with the British settlements. It appears that in the year 1819, a misunderstanding arose relative to an article in a treaty of peace negotiated by Mr. Bowdich in the year 1817 with the king of Ashantee. This misunderstanding, together with the imprudent and insolent conduct of the people of Cape Coast, led to a suspension of intercourse between its inhabitants and the Ashantees, who at first appeared very undecided as to their future intentions. Mr. Dupuis was, however, sent on a mission to Coomassie, and entered into a treaty with the monarch, acknowledging his claim to sovereignty on the Gold coast, and even the Fantee country, with the exception of the British jurisdiction within our factories, and their immediate vicinity. This treaty, however, was not ratified by the government at Cape Coast. Although Cape Coast was, from a kind of jealousy or suppressed hostility, shut out from participating in any mercantile intercourse with the interior, yet there was no demonstration of hostility on either side, nor was there, till August 1823, an Ashantee force within 100 m. of the coast. Affairs were pretty nearly in this situation when, in April 1822, Sir C. M'Carthy took possession of the forts along the Gold coast on behalf of Great Britain. Sir Charles seems to have come to a rather precipitate conclusion that the Ashantees were by no means formidable; at all events, he committed a grand error in not sending an embassy to assure the king of Ashantee of his friendly and pacific intentions. The nations on the Gold coast, a more intelligent race than any other tribes bordering upon our possessions in Africa, soon comprehended the nature of our policy, as explained by Sir Charles M'Carthy, whose name, as that of their deliverer from oppression, rung along the coast from Cape Apollonia to the mouth of the Volta; and the great influence which he gained over these people—the hitherto-acknowleged subjects of the king of A.—was viewed with indignation by that monarch. At last the impending storm burst, in the month of August 1822, by the seizure of a British sergeant, under some trivial pretence. This man was carried off to a Fantee town, named Donquah, about 18 m. inland from Annamaboa, and was detained there a prisoner, with the intention of finding out—as was afterwards learned—what part we should act in the endeavour to recover him. Sir Charles was at first unwilling to proceed to hostilities without knowing the real intentions of the Ashantees,—but negotiation was impracticable, for all intercourse between the Ashantees and the settlements had ceased since the seizure of the sergeant. At last, news arrived that the sergeant had been beheaded at Donquah on the 1st of Feb., 1823. An armed expedition to Donquah was now despatched, and the united forces of the Ashantees and Fantees defeated. The Ashantee monarch, however, nothing dismayed by the fortune of war, busied himself, by means of emissaries, in an attempt to intimidate our allies. In the beginning of June 1823, Sir Charles left Cape Coast on a tour of inspection to the Gambia and Sierra Leone. During his absence Captain Laing marched against the Ashantees, and repeatedly defeated them; but the arrival of Sir Charles M'Carthy, on the 28th of November, preceded by a detachment of white troops from England, did not infuse that spirit among the natives which every one had anxiously looked for. They had fancied that their great governor would have brought with him a ship load of guns, and an overwhelming host of white men; and their disappointment was great when he was seen to disembark from the colonial schooner with only two or three attendants. The king of A. was now in the field with an army of 15,000 men; and this force Sir Charles ventured to attack with about 1,000 men, but was defeated and killed. In 1826, an Ashantee army was signally defeated by a British force near Accra; and the king of A. immediately entered into a treaty of peace, which has hitherto been well observed.
ASH-BADWELL, a parish in Suffolk, 2 m. N of Great Ashfield. Area 2,000 acres. Pop. 458.
ASH-BOCKING, a parish in Suffolk, 6 m. E by S of Needham. Area 750 acres. Pop. 321.

ASHBORNE, or ASHBOURNE, a parish and market-town of Derbyshire, near the E side of the river Dove, 13 m. NW of Derby. It is a place of considerable manufacturing industry; and has several annual fairs. The church is ancient, and has been recently renovated. Area of p. 12,800 acres. Pop. 4,936. In the vicinity is Dovedale, whose romantic beauties are celebrated.
ASHBRITTLE, a parish in Somersetshire, 5 m. W of Wellington. Area 2,460 acres. Pop. 540. ASH
ASHBURGAN, a town of Great Bukharia, 45 m. W of Balkh, in N lat. 36° 44', E long. 64° 30'.

ASHBURNHAM, a township of Massachusetts, U. S., 53 m. NW of Boston, on Miller's river. Pop. 1,652.—Also a town and parish in Sussex, 9 m. from Hastings. Area 4,280 acres. Pop. 790. The family of Ashburnham take their title from this village.

ASHBURTON, a parish and parliamentary burgh in Devonshire, on the river Dart, 9 m. from Totness. Area of p., which is coterminous with the parl. burgh, 8,320 acres. Pop. 3,841. It sends one member to parliament. Here are mills for fulling and spinning yarn; and serge is manufactured here for the East India company. This town gives the title of baron to the Dunning family; and was the birth-place of W. Gifford, the first editor of the Quarterly Review.
ASHBURY, a parish in Berkshire, 6 m. NW of Lambourne. Area 5,520 acres. Pop. 619.—Also a p. in Devonshire, 5 m. SW of Hatherleigh. Area 1,650 acres. Pop. 65.

Pop. 429.—Also a parish in Norfolk, in the hund. of West Flegg. Area 1,150 acres. Pop. 16.—Also a p. in Loddon hund., in the same co. Area 640 acres. Pop. 263.—Also a p. in Suffolk, 5 m. NW of Lowestoft. Area 800 aeres. Pop. 53.
ASHBY-CANONS, a parish in Northamptonshire, 8 m. W by N of Towcester. Area 1,410 acres. Pop. 56.
ASHBY-CASTLE, a parish in Northamptonshire, 7 m. E by S of Northampton. Area 1,740 acres. Pop. 172.
ASHBY-COLD, a parish in Northamptonshire, 10 m. NNW of Northampton. Area 1,940 acres. Pop. 443.
ASHBY-CUM-FENBY, a parish in Lincolnshire, in Bradley-Haverstoe wapentake. Area 1,620 acres. Pop. 211.
ASHBY-FOLVILLE, a parish in Leicestershire, in the hund. of East Goscote. Area 2,830 acres. Pop. 437.
ASHBY-DE-LA-LAUND, a parish in Lincolnshire, in Flaxwell wapentake. Area 2,880 acres. Pop. 157.
ASHBY-ST.-LEDGER'S, a parish in Northampshire, 4 m. N of Daventry. Area 2,050 acres. Pop. 257.
ASHBY-MAGNA, a parish in Leicestershire, 4 m. N by E of Lutterworth. Area 1,720 acres. Pop. 505.
ASHBY-MEARS, a parish in Northamptonshire, in the hund. of Hamfordshoe. Area 1,890 acres. Pop. 496.
ASHBY-BY-PARTNEY, a parish in Lincolnshire, in the wapentake of Candleshoe. Area 1,210 acres. Pop. 160.
ASHBY-PARVA, a parish in Leicestershire, 3 m. N by W of Lutterworth. Area 1,260 acres. Pop. 179.
ASHBY-PUERORUM, a parish in Lincolnshire, 4 m. NE of Horneastle. Area 1,620 acres. Pop. 102.
ASHBY-WEST, a parish in Lincolnshire, 2 m. N of Horncastle. Area 1,590 acres. Pop. 534.

ASHBY-DE-LA-ZOUCH, a parish and markettown in Leicestershire, 18 m. NW by W of Leicester, and 10 m. E of the Burton-on-Trent station on the Birmingham and Derby Junction railway. Area of p. 8,300 acres. Pop. 5,652. The town has manufactures of malt, cotton, and hats. A large portion of it was destroyed by fire in 1753.—It is connected with Coventry by a canal 26 m. in length.
ASHCHURCH, a parish in Gloucestershire, 2 m. ENE of Tewkesbury. Area 4,240 acres. Pop. 743.
ASHCOMBE, a parish in Devenshire, 3 m. E of Chudleigh. Area 2,250 acres. Pop. 297.
ASHDOD, a town on the coast of Syria, 12 m. from Jaffa. It occupies the summit of a grassy hill, and is surrounded by a finely undulating country.
ASHDON, a parish and village in Essex, 4 m. NE of Saffron-Walden. Area 4,840 acres. Pop. 1,164.
ASHE, a co. in North Carolina, U. S., forming an elevated table-land of 840 sq. m., between Bald mountain and Blue ridge. Its cap. is Jefferson. Pop. 7,467.
ASHEIRA, a state of Guinea, 600 m. NE of Cape Lopez. It is watered by the Oguway.
ASHELDHAM, a parish in Essex, 4 m. SSW of Bradwell. Area 1,810 acres. Pop. 219.
ASHEN, a parish in Essex, 3 m. SW of Clare. Area 2,220 acres. Pop. 321.
ASHENDON, a parish in Bucks, 5 m. N of Thame. Area 1,790 acres. Pop. 312.—Also a hundred in the co. of Bucks, comprising 33 parishes. Area 66,670 acres. Pop. 13,416.
ASHEREF. See ASHRUFF.
ASHFIELD, a township in Franklin co., Massachusetts, U.S., 15 m. NW of Northampton. Pop. 1,610.
ASHFIELD-WITH-THORPE, a parish in Suffolk, 5 m. W by S of Framlingham. Area 1,560 acres. Pop. 343.
ASHFIELD (GREAT), a parish in Suffolk, 6 m. NW of Stowmarket. Area 2,030 acres. Pop. 396.

ASHFORD, a parish and town in Kent, 14 m. SW of Canterbury, on the river Stour. Area of p. 2,950 acres. Pop. 3,082. At the E end of the town is a bridge of 4 arches over the Stour. The South-eastern railway has a station here, 67 m distant from London, and 15 m. from Folkestone; and a branchline, 15 m. in length, runs off from the main trunk to Canterbury, through Wye and Godmersham. The SE railway company have a large locomotive establishment here. The town is one of the polling-places for the county, and gives the title of baron to the earl of Albemarle.—Also a p. in Devonshire, 2 m. NW of Barnstaple. Area, 2,170 acres. Pop. 174.—Also a p. in Middlesex, 3 m. E of Staines. Area 1,500 acres. Pop. 524.—Also a village of Derbyshire, on the Wye, 2 m. NW from Bakewell. Here are quarries of black marble, for the polishing of which there is a machine in the neighbourhood. Pop. 950.—Also a town in the co. of Galway, Ireland, in N lat. 53° 31', W long. 9° 16'.—Also a township in Windham co., Connecticut, U. S., 76 m. SW of Boston. Pop. 2,051.— Also a township in Cattarangus co., New York, U.S. Pop. 1,469.
ASHFORD (New), a township in Massachusetts, U. S., 158 m. W of Boston.
ASHFORD-BOWDLER, a parish in Salop, 3 m. S of Ludlow. Area 630 acres. Pop. 996.
ASHFORD-CARBONELL, a parish adjoining the above. Area 1,480 acres. Pop. 266.
ASHFORDBY, a parish in Leicestershire, 3 m. W of Melton-Mowbray. Area 1,210 acres. Pop. 482.
ASHGROVE, a township in King's co., Ireland, 25 m. WSW from Philipstown.
ASHIG, or Ashik, an island in the Red sea, 4 m. NNW from Toag.
ASHILL, a parish and village in Somersetshire, 3 m. NW of Ilminster. Area 1,860 acres. Pop. 438. -Also a p. in Norfolk, 31 m. NW of Watton. Area 2,970 acres. Pop. 637.
ASHINGDON, a parish in Essex, 3 m. N of Rochford. Area 1,020 acres. Pop. 119.
ASHING-KAY, a small low island in the Caribbean sea, off the Mosquito shore.
ASHINGTON, a parish in Somersetshire, 3 m. ESE of Ilchester. Area 550 acres. Pop. 71.—Also a p. in Sussex, 5 m. N of Steyning. Area 1,430 acres. Pop. 282.

ASHIPOO, a river in South Carolina, which rises in Charleston district, and running S by E, enters St. Helena sound by two channels, on the W side of ASH Fenwick's island. It has a course of 40 m.; and is navigable for boats 10 or 12 m.
ASHKIRK, a parish in the counties of Roxburgh and Selkirk, stretching along the river Ale. Pop. 563 in 1841; 597 in 1831.
ASHLEWORTH, a parish in Gloucestershire, in the hund. of Berkeley. Area 1,710 acres. Pop. 594.

ASHLEY, a parish in Northamptonshire, 5 m. WSW of Rockingham. Area 1,190 acres. Pop. 323.—Also a p. in Cambridgeshire, in the hund. of Cheveley. Area 2,143 acres. Pop. 417.—Also a township in Bowden p., Cheshire, 5 m. NE of Nether Knutsford. Area 2,390 acres. Pop. 377.-Also a p. in Hants, 3 m. SSE of Stockbridge. Area 2,580 acres. Pop. 102.—Also a p. in Staffordshire, 6 m. NW of Eccleshall. Area 2,600 acres. Pop. 853.— Also a p. in Wilts, 5 m. N of Malmesbury. Area 1,010 acres. Pop. 96.

ASHLEY, a river of West Florida, U. S., which falls into the gulf of Mexico, in N lat. 29° 58', W long. 84° 20'.—Also a river of South Carolina, U.S., which rises in the N part of Charleston district, runs SSE, and uniting with the Cooper on the SW side of Charleston, falls into the harbour E of the town. It is navigable for sloops and schooners several miles. On the W bank of this river is Old Charleston, where the first settlement in this state was made in 1671 by a colony under Governor Sayle.
ASHLEY-GREEN, a hamlet in Chesham p., Bucks, 21 m. NE of Chesham. Pop. 536.
ASHLEYHAY, a township in Wirksworth p., Derbyshire. Houses 53. Pop. 272.
ASHLEY-MILTON, a tything in Milton p., Hants. Pop. 552. Houses 126.
ASHLEY (North), a tything in Ringwood p., Hants. Houses 37. Pop. 237.
ASHLING (East and West), two tythings in Funtington p., Sussex. Pop. of East A. 310; of West A. 455.
ASHMANHAUGH, a parish in Norfolk, 3 m. E by N of Coltishall. Area 700 acres. Pop. 180.
ASHMANSWORTH, a parish in Hants, 8 m. NW of Whitchurch. Area 1,740 acres. Pop. 220.
ASHMORE, a parish in Dorset, 5 m. SE of Shaftesbury. Area 3,940 acres. Pop. 242.
ASHMUN, a village of Lower Egypt, near the r. bank of the Rosetta branch of the Nile, 25 m. NW of Cairo.—Also a v. on a branch of the Nile which runs into Lake Menzaleh.
ASHMUNEIN, a town of Central Egypt, on the W side of the Nile, 120 m. S of Cairo, on the site of the ancient Hermopolis. Pop. 5,000. A. was in Abulfeda's time a large city.
ASHNUR, a large village of Hindostan, in the prov. of Ajmeer, district of Harroti. Pop. 1,200. The soil of the surrounding territory is rich, and highly cultivated.
ASHOLT, a parish of Somersetshire, 6 m. W by S of Bridgewater. Area 2,650 acres. Pop. 201.
ASHOVER, a parish and village in Derbyshire, 6 m. WNW of Chesterfield. Area of p. 11,290 acres. Pop. 3,482. In the neighbourhood there is a remarkable rocking-stone, and some other Druidical remains.
ASHOW, a parish in Warwickshire, 4 m. NNE of Warwick. Area 1,100 acres. Pop. 172.
ASHPERTON, a chapelry in Hertfordshire, 5 m. NW of Ledbury. Area 1,090 acres. Pop. 604.
ASHPRINGTON, a parish in Devonshire, 3 m. SE of Totness. Area 2,240 acres. Pop. 588.
ASHREIGNY, a parish in Devonshire, 4 m. W by S of Chumleigh. Area 6,220 acres. Pop. 1,088.

ASHRUFF, a town of Persia in Mazanderan, near the coast of the Caspian, 40 m. E by N of Sari, 60 m. W of Astrabad. The great Shah Abbas had a splendid establishment here, which is described by Hanway as it appeared at a time when it still retained much of its original splendour, and the ruins of which, as existing in 1822, are described by Mr. J. B. Fraser. A. now contains only about 500 houses thinly scattered through an extensive jungle.

ASHTA, a pergunnah and town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Malwa, 59 m. E by S of Ujein, on the E bank of the Parbutty.—Also a v. in the prov. of Bejapur, near Punderpur, in the vicinity of which the Peishwa was defeated in 1818.

ASHTABULA, a co. in Ohio, U. S., on the height of land between Lake Erie and the Ohio. The co. is 32 m. long, and 25 m. broad. Pop. 23,724 in 1840. -Also a town on Lake Erie, in the NE part of the co. of A. It has a good harbour at the mouth of a stream of the same name about 30 m. in length, which flows into Lake Erie.
ASHTEAD, a parish and village in Surrey, 2 m. SW from Epsom. Area 2,510 acres. Pop. 618.

ASHTOLA, an uninhabited island about 12 m. from the coast of Mekrán, in the Persian gulf. It is 2 m. long. Its cliffs rise abruptly from the sea to a height of 300 ft. It is frequented by fine turtle.

ASHTON, a township in Tarvin p., Cheshire, 8 m. ENE of Chester. Pop. 401.—Also a p. in Devon, 41 m. N by W of Chudleigh. Area 1,560 acres. Pop. 319.—Also a tything in Bishop's Waltham p., Hants. Pop. 310.—Also a p. in Northamptonshire, 7 m. S of Northampton. Area 1,290 acres. Pop. 417.—Also a hamlet in Oundle p., Northamptonshire. Pop. 172.—Also a hamlet in Ufford p., Northamptonshire. Pop. 101.
ASHTON-COLD, a parish in Gloucestershire, 10 m. E by S of Bristol. Area 2,310 acres. Pop. 414.
ASHTON-GIFFORD, a township in Codford p., Wilts, 6 m. SE of Warminster. Pop. 141.
ASHTON-UNDER-HILL, a parish in Gloucestershire, 5 m. SW of Evesham. Area 1,300 acres. Pop. 342.
ASHTON-KEYNES, a parish in Wiltshire, 4 m. W of Cricklade. Area 3,820 acres. Pop. 1,332.
ASHTON (Long), a parish in Somersetshire, 3 m. SW of Bristol. Area 4,110 acres. Pop. 1,926.

ASHTON-UNDER-LYNE, a p. and incorp. b.town in the hund. of Salford, co.-palatine of Lancaster, governed by a mayor, 8 aldermen, and 24 councillors. The p., which is 6 m. in length, and 4 m. in breadth, contains the divs. of Ashton-under-Lyne, Audenshaw, Hartshead, and Knott-Lanes. The district is remarkable for general levelness, a cold and marshy soil, and immense coal-deposits, employing upwards of 1,000 men. The cotton-mills in 1850 were 81, employing 3,133 horse-power, and 12,639 hands, besides 1 silk manufactory, employing 260 hands. The A. poor-law union comprises 13 townships in the cos. of Chester and Lancaster, with a population in 1841 of 101,570. Expenditure in 1841, £9,179; in 1849, £14,762. Average of three years prior to the union, £6,098. Pop. of the parish in 1715, 5,097; in 1801, 15,632; in 1841, 46,304. Houses in 1841, 8,254. Acres 9,300. The annual value of real property assessed to the property and incometax in 1842-3, was as follows: Ashton, £84,716; Audenshaw, £15,503; Hartshead, £44,688; Knott- Lanes, £30,509; total, £175,416. The town of A.—so called from its being situated near to or under the remarkable line of hills called 'the Backbone of England'-is situated in N lat. 53° 29', W long. 2° 5', on an eminence rising from 30 to 40 ft. above the N bank of the Tame, 186 m. NW of London; 61 m. by railway ENE of of Samarcand. ASH Manchester; and 4 m. S of Oldham. The principal manufactures of this place are connected with the cotton trade, which had its rise in 1769. In 1844, there were 595,276 mule spindles, 9,000 throstle spindles, 6,738 power-looms, 1,667 horse-power, and 10,520 hands employed in the cotton trade; and the weekly consumption of cotton was estimated at 338,390 lbs. The amount of weekly wages in this trade was £5,775; being 10s. 113d. per head. The present manufactories at Ashton, Dukinfield, and Stalybridge, are chiefly employed in spinning the finer kinds of yarn, and in weaving by power-looms plain twilled and figured calicoes, and the finer decriptions of printing-cloths. A. is admirably situated both for trade and manufacture. Coals are plentiful; by the Tame, canals, and mill-owners' water-works, water in abundance is supplied; and three of the c. of Yorkshire, Derbyshire, and Lancashire, centre here. By the A. canal, merchandise is conveyed to Manchester, and from thence to Liverpool; by the Huddersfield c. a water-conveyance is opened to the German ocean; and by the Peak-forest c., a communication is made to the heart of Derbyshire, for the conveyance of fuel and the reception of lime. The Manchester, Sheffield, and Lincolnshire railway passes near the town, on the S; and a branch-railway, 2 m. 44½ chains in length, has been executed between it and A. and Stalybridge. At its point of junction at Guide-bridge, 4 m. 74 chains from the terminus in Manchester, and 3281 ft. above sea-level, the London and North-western railway branch from Stockport unites. A branch of the Lancashire and Yorkshire railway, commencing at Miles-Platting, near Manchester, passes through A. on the N side—61 m. from Manchester—to Stalybridge—7 m. from Manchester-where it joins the London and North-western railway, by which line direct communication is obtained with Huddersfield, and the W. R. of Yorkshire. The town was anciently a burgh; but for some cause not well-ascertained it was disfranchised. It was again incorporated by charter from the privy council in Sept. 1847; and is one of the new burghs which return a member under the reform bill. The pop. of the parliamentary burgh in 1841 was 22,514. The number of electors in 1842-3 was 730. The pop. of the town in 1821 was 9,222; in 1831, 14,673. The ratio of deaths to pop. in 1841 was 1 in 32.4; of births, 1 in 27.6; of ill. children to total births, 1 in 12.8. Mr, Coulthart calculates the average mortality of A. at 3.02 per cent.; while that of England and Wales is 2.19 per cent.; and the average height of the whole adult male inhabitants at 5 ft. <math>6\frac{3}{4}</math> inches, while that of Englishmen in general is 5 ft. 9 inches.

ASHTON-IN-MACKERFIELD, or ASHTON-INTHE-WILLOWS, a township and chapelry in the parish of Winwick, co.-palatine of Lancaster, 2½ m. NW of Newton, near the line of the Wigan railway. There are some collieries and cotton manufactories in the neighbourhood. Pop., including that of Haydock township, in 1801, 3,696; in 1831, 5,912; in 1841, 6,706. Houses in 1841, 1,198.

ASHTON-ON-MERSEY, a parish and township in the hund. of Bucklow, co.-palatine of Chester, 3 m. NE of Altrincham. Pop. of the township, in 1801, 778; in 1841, 1,105. Houses in 1841, 219. Acres 1,630. Pop. of the parish, including the township of Sale, in 1841, 2,414. Houses 460. Acres 3,690.
ASHTON (POINT), a cape on the W coast of North America, in N lat. 53° 50', W long. 128° 53'.
ASHTON-STEEPLE, a parish in Wilts, including the tythings of Ashton-Steeple, Ashton-West, Littleton, and Hinton, with the chapelry of Semington. Area 7,450 acres. Pop. 1,941.
ASHTRIAN, a town of Great Bukharia, 25 m. W
ASHUEK, a town of Asiatic Turkey, in the prov. of Irak, 95 m. NNW of Bagdad.

ASHUELOT, a river of the United States in New Hampshire, formed by several streams which flow from ponds in Cheshire co., and unite in Swansey co. It flows from S to W, and joins the Connecticut river at Hinsdale.
ASHUFU, a town of Africa, in the Ashantee territory, 102 m. NNE of Cape Coast Castle.
ASHUMA, one of the Petshu group in the Eastern sea, in N lat. 24° 40', E long. 124° 45'.
ASHURST, a parish in Kent, on the borders of Sussex; 3 m. W of Tunbridge-Wells. Area 1,000 acres. Pop. 224.—Also a parish in Sussex, 3 m. N of Steyning. Area 2,250 acres. Pop. 427.
ASHWATER, a parish in Devon, 6 m. SE by E of Holsworthy. Area 4,220 acres. Pop. 1,046. The Exeter and Falmouth railway passes through this parish.

ASHWELL, a parish and village in Herts, 4 m. NNE of Baldock. Area 3,500 acres. Pop. 1,235. -Within the parish, about half-a-mile from the village, is an entrenchment called Arbury banks, supposed to be the remains of a Roman fortification, in the neighbourhood of which various Roman antiquities have been found.—Also a parish in Rutland, 3½ m. N from Oakham. Area 1,890 acres. Pop. 223.
ASHWELL-THORPE, a parish in Norfolk, 3½ m. SE from Wymondham. Area 1,030 acres. Pop. 469.
ASHWICK, a parish in Somerset, 3 m. N by E from Shepton-Mallet. Area 1,700 acres. Pop. 945.
ASHWICKEN, or ASHWYKEN, a parish in Norfolk, 5 m. ESE from King's Lynn. Area 1,260 acres. Pop. 78.
ASHWORTH, a chapelry in the parish of Middleton, co.-palatine of Lancaster; 3 m. W from Rochdale, on a branch of the Roch. Area 730 acres. Pop. 325.
ASI, a river of Italy, which rises in the Apennines, and falls into the Tiber near Orvieto.

ASIA. ONE of the great divisions of the globe. In extent of surface it is superior to America in the proportion of 8 to 6 nearly; and it exceeds Europe and Africa taken together; while in antiquity of history and of civilization, in amount of population, in diversity and beauty of surface, in richness of soil, and in the variety of its productions, it surpasses all the other divisions. It was in Asia that the first families of mankind had their primitive seats, and from Asia that arts and civilization were diffused over the other regions of the earth. To Christians it likewise presents features of the highest interest as containing the principal scenes of sacred history. Name.] The name Asia is very ancient; but its origin is uncertain. Homer, Herodotus, and Euripides, apply it to a district of Lydia, occupied by a tribe called Asiones, who possessed a city which they called Asia; and—as Malte Brun has observed in his 'Precis'—the Greeks may have gradually extended this designation, originally given to a small district of Asia Minor, to the whole of the Asiatic continent as they became acquainted with it. It was thus that the French extended the name of the duchy of Allemagne to the whole of Germany; and that the ancient canton of Italia, in a remote corner of Calabria, imposed its name on the great peninsula of which it forms so inconsiderable a portion. The ancients appear to have sometimes comprehended Egypt within the limits of Asia. Many conjectures have been hazarded regarding the origin of the word Asia. Some authors-among whom is the very learned Bochart-consider it to be of Phœnician or Hebrew origin, and to signify 'the middle;' this continent being, according to the ideas of the ancients, placed between the two other known continents of the world. Others have traced its origin to the Sanscrit, and assert that it must have been originally used to signify 'the East,' or the land in which the morning-light first dawns. The ancient Greeks-who boldly account in their mythology for the origin of all geographical names-derive the name from that of the wife of Prometheus. Herodotus says that the Lydians supposed it to have been derived from Asius, son of Cotys, son of Manes, who also gave his name to the Ases, or Ασιαδα, a Sardian tribe. Boundaries.] On the NW, A. is separated from Europe only by an imaginary line, the course of which is variously traced by different geographers. The boundary usually assigned to A. in this quarter is the Arabian gulf, and the isthmus of Suez, which separate it from Africa; the Mediterranean and Ægean seas; the Hellespont, and the Propontis; the Bosphorus of Thrace; the Euxine or Black sea, the straits of Kaffa, the sea of Azoff, the Don, the Wolga to its confluence with the Samara; and thence by an irregular line running still NE to the Uralian mountains; then the Urals themselves, to the sources of Petshora; thence to the mouth of the Kara, and across to Nova Zembla. Others draw the boundary line from the straits of Kaffa, up the Kuban and down the Terek river to the Caspian sea; along the NW shore of that sea to the mouth of the Jaik; up that stream to the Ural mountains, and thence to the Icy sea. These boundaries assigned to Asia in the 'Dictionnaire Geographique' of Paris, cut off a great part of what is usually called Asia, as all the region to the N of the Caucasus, from the Don to the Jaik, -a region including the governments of Caucasus and Daghistan, that of Astrakhan, and most of the government of Orenburg, comprehending an area of more than 300,000 sq. m. Asia is likewise connected with Africa by the isthmus of Suez, which is only 48 geographical miles broad; but excepting at these two points, it is everywhere surrounded by the sea. On its NE extremity it is divided from America by Behring's straits. On the N it is bounded by the Icy ocean, which here forms a series of obscure gulfs and promontories extending throughout the vast space of 130 degrees of longitude, but of which little knowledge has yet been obtained. Its boundary on the E from Behring's straits to the straits of Malacca, is the Pacific ocean, under the names of the sea of Kamtschatka, the sea of Japan, the Yellow sea, the Chinese sea, &c. On the S it is bounded by the Indian ocean, under a variety of names derived from the different coasts which it laves, and which here forms the two great Indian peninsulas. The Arabian gulf, the isthmus of Suez, the Mediterranean, the Grecian archipelago, the Dardanelles, the sea of Marmora, the straits of Constantinople, the Black sea, and a part of the imaginary line already noticed, may be considered as forming the western boundaries of Asia. Extent and square superficies.] This continent is situated between the 26th and the 190th meridian of E long., and between the 2d and 77th parallels of N lat. A line drawn from East cape at Behring's straits, to the entrance of the Arabian gulf, or perhaps to the straits of the Dardanelles, would denote the greatest extent of this continent, and would measure above 7,500 m.; while a line drawn from Cape Severo-vostochnoi in Siberia, under the parallel of 76° 10' N, to Cape Romania, the southern extremity of Malacca, in 1° 18' N, would measure nearly 4,500 geographical, or 5,166 British m. Supposing its mean extent from E to W to be about 100 degrees, or 4,275 geographical m., and from N to S 60 degrees, or 3,600 geographical m., its superficial area will be 15,390,000 geographical sq. m. Hassel has calculated it from Arrowsmith's map of 1818 at 20,432,205 English sq. m.; and Gräberg estimates it, without the islands of the Indian ocean, at 16,262,100 sq. m. Berghaus assigns to the continental part of A. 810,000 German sq. m., and to the islands 72,600 sq. m.; or 882,600 German sq. m. <math>= 18,975,900</math> English sq. m. in all. The fact however is, all these admeasurements necessarily are mere approximations; and none of them can be regarded as presenting any claim to perfect accuracy Great central plateau.] The physical features of this vast continent, the courses of its rivers, the direction of its chains of mountains, the various climates of its different regions, and the relative situations of its different members or limbs, will be best understood by attending, in the first place, to the great central plateau of A. The central regions of this continent rise into a vast and highly elevated plain, which extends some thousands of miles in every direction, and is flanked on all sides by high and precipitous mountains, which overlook the surrounding countries, and some of which rise to the height of 28,000 ft. These mountains form four lines enclosing an irregular-sided figure; they meet on the NW, NE, SE, and SW; and if we suppose the extreme points joined together by right lines, these lines would enclose a four-sided figure, divided into two triangles of nearly equal sides by a diagonal line drawn from NW to SE. It is from this elevated table-land-comprising, according to Humboldt's admeasurement, 3,226,000 geographical sq. m.—that the rivers of Asia flow, as from a common centre, to the surrounding seas. The northern flank of this plateau, generally known as the Altai range, commences on the NW on the E banks of the Irtish in about lat. 50° N, E long. 80° E, and extends eastwards, in various imperfectly known ranges, and under different names, to the S extremity of the sea of Okhotsk; but the NE extremity of the great plateau may perhaps be fixed in the mountainknot of Kentei in lat. 48° N, long. 110° E. To the N of this great northern bulwark of the central high land the surface subsides into the steppes of Siberia, and descends almost without interruption to the shores of the Icy sea. The eastern flank, beginning at the mountains of Kentei, runs ESE to the great chain of In-shan, or the mountains of Siolki, along which it runs first SW and then S to the 40th parallel. From this point it takes a SW direction. To speak with precision of the eastern, and likewise of the southern flanks, so far as they respect China Proper and the Birman empire, is impossible for want of accurate data. As far as we can rely on the maps of China, by Du Halde and others, the eastern flank of the great plateau in this part commences on the frontier of Shansee; in the Kuen-lun or Kwang-lung, runs S till it touches the northern frontier of Sifan in 33° N lat.; thence bends S and SW along the frontier of Sifan, to the 30th parallel, where it strikes the Yalong river in 101° E long., and thence runs S to the point where the Yalong enters China in 101° E long. and 28° N lat. The eastern face of the Tibetian plateau, from the NE frontier of the Sifan, in 33° N lat., to this point, is called in Chinese Swee-Shan or 'the Country of snow.' From 28° N lat. and 101° E long., the flank of the plateau runs SW in an irregular line, crossing the points where the Kincha-Kiang, the Matchu and the Lu-Kiang, successively leave the Alpine country of Tibet and enter Yunnan in China. At this last point, in 26° N lat. and 98° 25' E long., commences the southern flank of the Tibetian plateau. From the NE angle of the Birman dominions, where the mountains meet those of Tibet and China, the southern flank runs on till it reaches the angle formed by the junction of the snowy chain of the Langtang with the main range, in 28° N lat. and 97° 25' E long. From this point, the southern flank continues due W to the pass of Phari, in 89° E long. and 27° 58' N lat., whence it takes a WNW course to the pass of Niti, in 31° N lat. and 80° E long. It then runs WNW, shutting up the valleys of the Sutluj and Spiti from Northern Hindostan, and passing onwards to the eastern frontier of Cashmere, where sweeping to the NNW and around Cashmere, it crosses the Indus in 35° 30' N lat. and 75° E long.; and runs W, | join a branch of the Sungarian mountains on the E in a very waving line, till it joins the Belur-Tagh, in 71° E long. and 34° 30' N lat., which in its turn becomes the western flank of the great plateau. But it must be observed that though this point of junction forms the NW angle of the southern flank, the Himalaya still pursues an unbroken western course as far as the pass of Bamian, 50 m. W of the great snowy peak of the Hindukush, where the snowy chain terminates, and is lost in the Paropamisan mountains, in 67° 40' E long. and 35° N lat. From 34° 30' N lat. and 71° E long. the Belur holds a NE course to the source of the Oxus, in 73° E long. and 38° N lat. according to Elphinstone's map. Thence it runs NNW to its junction with the NW termination of the Muz-Tagh; after which, under the name of the Sung-ling, or 'Azure mountains,' it forms the western boundary of Eastern Turkistan, till it meets the western extremity of the Alak-Tagh, or 'Speckled mountain,' the Tin-Shan, or 'Celestial mountain' of the Chinese. In this part of its course, the Belur is denominated by the Tartars Terek-Daban. From this point, under the name of Aidzin, it makes two curves, first to the NE, and then to the NW, after which it joins the Jimbal mountains, passing to the W of the Balkasch-Nur. The western flank then runs N and joins the Kichik-Tagh, which, according to Rennel's map, joins the Ulugh-Tagh in 75° E long. and 48° 30' N lat. It is on this side that the countries which formed the ancient Persian empire, and the Kirghissian and Carismian steppes, descend towards the Caspian sea. The plateau, whose boundaries we have thus imperfectly traced, may be generally described as composed of an assemblage of naked mountains, enormous rocks, and lofty plains. It is subdivided into a number of secondary basins, the position and extent of which are determined by the mountain-ridges which intersect its surface. The principal of these ridges are: the Great Altai, which takes a SE direction, and the mountains of Sungaria, which run off from the point of junction of the Adjagu-Tagh and the Chamar-Daban, and take a SW direction to the parallel of 46° 50'. The latter turn ESE to about E long. 86°, and then running ENE join the Great Altai in 46° N lat. and 96° E long. They surround the lakes of Alatagul, Ajan, and Kosilbash, forming a basin for these bodies of water with the great northern flank of the plateau. To the NE of this great basin we perceive the lakes of Eke-aral and Upsa, which are formed by the Great Altai, a part of the northern flank of the plateau, and a chain from the Khanggai. The Tien-Shan mountains running ENE join the mountains of Sungaria, and form the S and SE boundaries of the basin of the lake of Balkash, which is bounded on the W by the western flank of the plateau, and on the E by the Sungarian chain. Several considerable rivers, among which are the Ili, discharge themselves into this lake. A branch of the latter chain, striking off in 90° E long., runs SSE until it reaches the eastern flank of the plateau, with which it forms the basin of Lake Lop, which is bounded on the S by the Muz-Tagh. This is the largest basin of the central plateau, and comprehends the courses of a number of large rivers, such as the Khotan, the Jarkiang, the Cashgar, and the Dochkan. Between this basin and the southern flank of the plateau are two basins divided from each other by the mountains of Khor which run E and W. The most northerly of these is that of the lake Namur, the other is the basin of the Tengri-nur. The mountains of Nomkhun or Urgan-Tagh, which detach themselves from the Great Altai in 97° E long., and run SE to the In-shan, and the mountains of Tangut, which quit the Nomkhun under the 44th parallel, and of the basin of Lake Lop, enclose a number of inconsiderable lakes.
