Mount Ararat, 17,280 Himalaya chain, 28,000 Mountains and Basins of Northern A.] From the NW angle of the great plateau, a chain of mountains runs out, under the name of Ulugh-Tagh, as far as the 50th parallel and 72d meridian. It then turns W to 69° E long., whence it runs SW, and joins the mountains of Kirghiz or Algydim-Zano under the 49th parallel. These latter run WNW to 51° N lat. and 61° 10' E long. Thus far we may regard the chain whose course we are now tracing as forming a common boundary betwixt Northern and Southern Asia; and thereafter, continued in the Ural chain directly N till it reaches the Icy ocean, as dividing Northern Asia from Europe in the latter part of its course. Between the mountain-line now traced, and that which runs out from the NE corner of the great plateau, there occur three chains of mountains, all running northwards, and forming, with the extreme NW and NE chains, four great basins: these are the mountains of Kutznetz, the Baikal mountains, and the mountains of Aldan. The mountains of Kutznetz diverge from the Little Altai, under the 51st parallel, and run NNE, forming the eastern limit of the basin of the Obi, which is bounded on the S by a part of the northern flank of the great plateau, and the mountains of Kirghiz, and on the W by the Ural chain. This eastern limit of the basin of the Obi forms also the western limit of the basin of the Jenisei, which is bounded on the E by the Baikal chain, which strikes off from the Daourian mountains, under the 52d parallel, and runs first N, and then SW, around the Baikal lake, and then N to Cape Severo-vostochnoi, after having separated the sources of the Lena from those of the Jenisei. The eastern and western extremities of the Little Altai are comprehended in the basins of the Jenisei and Obi. Its summits are here covered with perpetual snow, and cannot be lower than the Alps, which are nearly in the same latitude. The basin of the Lena includes the two smaller basins of the Olenek and Anabara. The Aldan mountains, which strike off from the Lamutian mountains in the NE part of the northern flank | of the great plateau, and under the parallel of 63°, and which stretch to the 132d meridian, separate the basin of the Lena from that of the Indijirka, and the Kovima, which is bounded on the E by that part of the chain which, striking off from the NE point of the great plateau, terminates at Behring's straits. Mountains and Basins of Eastern A.] A chain strikes off from the NE angle of the great plateau, in 48° N lat. and 110° E long., and separates Eastern Siberia from Chinese Tartary. It first runs NW to the 50th parallel; then turns NE in the Daourian mountains, which run in the same direction as far as the 53d parallel, where they bend E to the 121st meridian. At this latter point they reach the mountains of Khinggan, which run NNE to 56° 40' N lat., and thence ESE to the Lamutes. These latter heights run NE along the sea of Okhotsk as far as the 61st parallel, where they turn NNW to 62° 30' N lat. From this point they return ESE along the borders of the sea of Okhotsk to 172° 10' E long., whence they run ENE to Behring's straits, terminating in East cape, the most easterly point of the Asiatic continent. This chain generally takes the name of Stanovoi after passing the 130th meridian.-Eastern Asia is divided by its mountain-system into five great basins, corresponding with its five principal seas. The most northern basin belongs to the sea of Behring. It is bounded on the NE by the chain we have just described, and on the opposite side by the mountains of Kamtschatka, which diverge from that of the sea of Okhotsk. The basin of the sea of Okhotsk is bounded on the N and W by the Lamutes, and on the S by a chain which detaches itself at the point of junction common to the Lamutes and the mountains of Khinggan.—The basin of the sea of Japan is bounded on the N by the Khinggan mountains and the Daourian chain, and on the S by the mountains of In-shan, and a chain which, detaching itself from them in 119° E long., and 46° N lat., is prolonged in the Shanyin-Alin to the extremity of the peninsula of Corea. Submarine ramifications are supposed to unite this chain to the mountains in the islands of Japan, Jesso, and Tchoka or Sagalin, which seem connected on the other side with the mountains of Khinggan. The basin which we have now described comprehends the basins of various rivers of which the principal is the Amur.—The mountains which form the southern boundary of the basin of the sea of Japan, likewise form the northern boundary of the basin of the Yellow sea, or sea of Corea, which is bounded on the W by the eastern flank of the great plateau, from which the mountains of Sianpi detach themselves in 115° E long. The basin of the sea of Corea is divided into the two inferior basins of the Hoang-Ho or Yellow river, and the Yang-Tse-Kiang river. The line of demarcation betwixt the tributaries of these two rivers is formed by the northern portion of the Baian-Khara mountains, which run out from the eastern flank of the great plateau, in 35° N lat. and 95° E long., at the point where the Hoang-Ho and Yang-Tse-Kiang have their sources. This line is continued in the Pe-ling mountains which run eastwards to the Chinese sea, after having sent off the mountains of Shansi which force the Hoang-Ho to take their direction until it encounters the southern ramifications of the In-shan mountains. The Pe-ling also sends off to the S the Yun-ling which forces the Yang-Tse-Kiang to make a considerable circuit.—The mountains of Amdoa separate the basin of the Yellow sea on the W and S from that of the Chinese sea. These mountains run from the sources of the Yang-Tse-Kiang along the right bank of that river as far as the 24th parallel and the 102d meridian. At this point, the mountains of Nan-ling detach themselves, and bending NE under the name of Yang-ling, run nearly to the Chinese sea .- The range running off from the E point of the great plateau forms the western boundary of the basin of the Chinese sea. This great basin is divided into four river-basins, marked out by the Ma-tian-ling, which runs off from the Nan-ling under the 25th parallel,—the mountains of An-nam, a prolongation of those of Amdoa,—and a branch of the mountains of Siam. The most important of these four basins are that of the Meikong which opens into the eastern point of the gulf of Siam, and that of the Meinam which is also connected with the same gulf. The other two basins are that of the Sikiang on the N, and of the Sang-koi belonging to the gulf of Tonquin. Mountains and Basins of Southern A.] From the SE point of the great plateau, a ridge of mountains runs SE to the mountains of Wei, which remount to the NE, but send out a branch to the SE, under the 27th parallel. The mountains of Siam may be considered as the prolongation of this line to the extremity of the peninsula of Malacca. Southern A. may be viewed as comprehending four great basins, one of which is the basin of a gulf, another an interior basin, and the remaining two maritime basins. The streams which flow into the gulf of Bengal determine the first of these; the second embraces the Persian plateau; the third contains the streams which flow towards the sea of Oman; and the fourth comprehends all the coast washed by the Mediterranean. The basin of the gulf of Bengal is formed on the E by the chain just described, which separates Eastern from Southern A.; on the N its boundary is a part of the southern flank of the great plateau. On the W its limits are marked by a chain which detaches itself, in 81° 40' E long., from that point in the central plateau where the Sind and the Sampo rivers have their sources, and which running S, reaches the Himalayas under the 30th parallel. From the latter mountains it stretches southward to the mountains of Vindhya, in which it runs E, giving birth to the principal sources of the Jumna. Turning SW, in the mountains of Berar, it reaches the Western Ghauts under the 20th parallel; these, running along the W coast of the western peninsula of India, terminate in Cape Comorin, and complete the outline of the basin of the gulf of Bengal, which is divided into three inferior basins. On the E is the basin of the Irawaddy; in the centre is the basin of the Ganges, which receives the Brahmaputra; the western basin receives such streams as flow towards the gulf of Bengal from the Eastern Ghauts. The western limit of the basin of the gulf of Bengal forms the eastern boundary of the basin of the sea of Oman. To this latter basin belong not only the Sind, and the Nerbudda, and the torrents which descend from the Western Ghauts, but likewise all the streams which flow into the Persian and Arabian gulfs, from the Arabian plateau. We may indeed regard the great basin of the gulf of Oman as divided into three gulf-basins. The first of these is the basin of the gulf of Oman, properly so called, of which we have already traced the eastern boundaries in those of the great basin itself,—and of which the western boundaries are the mountains of Afghanistan and Brahuick, which detach themselves under the 71st meridian from the southern side of the Hindukush, and descend in a southern direction to the coast, giving off, on the right bank of the Sind, the high mountains of Soliman. This basin is again subdivided into several minor basins, of which the principal are the basins of the Sind, the Nerbudda, and the Tapty. The second basin is that of the Persian gulf, into which the Jemamah, the Tigris, and the Euphrates, discharge themselves; and all those streams which descend from the mountains of Mekran, a subsidiary ridge of the Brahuick mountains, and the mountains of Bakhtiauri, a continuation of the Sagros. In the third basin, included in the great basin of the gulf of Oman, we comprehend the southern and western coasts of Arabia, and might also include the western coasts of the gulf of Arabia, which, however, although they complete the outline of this basin, belong to the African continent. The interior basin of Southern Asia—that is to say, the Persian plateau—is bounded by the Elburz and the mountains of Khorasan on the N; by the Afghanistan and Beluchistan mountains on the E; on the S by those of Mekran; and on the W by the mountains of Bakhtiauri. The lake Zurrah forms the centre of this plateau. The only mountain-range of any consequence belonging to it, is the Wushuti chain, a ramification of the southern part of the mountains of Beluchistan. The fourth basin, that of the Mediterranean, comprehends all the streams which descend from the Taurus and Libanus towards that sea. Watered by numerous and large rivers, and having its rich soil finely exposed to the sun, Southern A. exhibits a striking contrast to the gloomy and ice-bound solitudes of the northern part of this vast continent. Mountains and Basins of Western A.] The main chain which runs out from the SW corner of the central plateau, runs directly W in the Hindukush and the mountains of Khorasan. The latter turning N in 59° long., join the Elburz. The Elburz chain runs WSW, and then NW, sending off a range which joins the Elvend on the SSW, which again is united on the NW with Ararat. The main line of the Elburz, however, continues a NW course to the Tchildir mountains, and is continued towards the SW by the chain in which the Ardjieh-Dagh occurs. At this latter point it turns SE, and joins the Taurus under the parallel of 38° 15'. With the Taurus it runs SW to 37° 10' N lat. and 33° 10' E long., and thence passes to Mount Olympus, whence it runs NNE to the straits of Constantinople which divide it from the Balkan system of European Turkey. Three great basins, each belonging to inland seas, present themselves in Western A. These are the basins of the sea of Aral, of the Caspian, and of the Black sea .- The basin of the Aral is a deep plain, bounded on the N by the Kirghissian mountains; on the E by the Asiatic plateau; on the S by the Hindukush and the mountains of Khorasan; and on the W by a ramification of the Kirghissian chain. The Sihon river flows through the centre of this basin, and the Gihon has its source on the SW point of the great plateau. The basin of the Aral includes the basin of the lake of Kaban-Koulac, into which the Sarasu discharges itself.—The NW portion of the basin of the Caspian is considered by some geographers—whom we do not follow-as belonging to Europe. The Tedjen, the Kizil-ozan, and the Kur rivers belong to this basin. -Of the basin of the Black sea, a part belongs to Europe. This basin is divided into a number of inferior basins. All the streams which descend from the northern side of Taurus, from the mountains of Tchildir, and from the NW side of Caucasus, discharge themselves into the Black sea; but the most remarkable streams are the Kizil-Irmak and the Phasis, whose basin is formed by ramifications of the Taurus. The seas which border Western A. give to it some resemblance to a great peninsula. It is decidedly opposed in physical characteristics to Eastern A. The latter is in general moist,—the former is a dry, and in some places an arid region; Eastern A. has a stormy and very often a cloudy sky,-Western A. enjoys constant breezes and great serenity of atmosphere; the one has chains of steep mountains, separated by marshy plains,—the other is composed of sandy plateaus, little inferior in elevation to the mountains which rise out of them; in Eastern A. we see long rivers pursuing courses near to each other, -whilst in Western A. there are only two or three rivers of any considerable size, but, as a sort of compensation, there are numerous lakes without any outlet. Lastly, the proximity of the burning deserts of Africa communicate to a great part of Western A. a temperature much above that which Southern A. enjoys. Volcanoes.] Although A. contains few volcanoes in proportion to its extent, yet many are in a constant state of activity throughout this great continent, and even in the central plateau itself; while some, known to have blazed in former times, are now quiet, though smoke still issues or hot streams are discharged from crevices on their sides. In Kamtschatka there are several high volcanic summits in active operation constituting the extreme northern Asiatic portion of a great volcanic belt or line which appears to traverse in its progress southwards the Kurile islands, the islands of Japan, the Philippine islands, and the Sunda islands, and to approach New Zealand through New Guinea, the New Hebrides, and the Friendly islands; while it is prolonged at the other extremity to the American continent through the Aleutian chain, many of whose summits are in a state of volcanic activity. The loftiest volcano in Kamtschatka is the Kliutchefskaia, in N lat. 58° 04′ 18", E long. 160° 30′ 48", which has an alt. of 15,825 ft., or 15,763 ft., according to other admeasurements. Next to it is the Uchinskaja, in N lat. 56° 00′ 30″, E long. 160° 17′, alt. 11,721 ft.; the Koriazkaja, alt. 11,210 ft.; the Kronozkaja, alt. 10,604 ft.; and the NE summit of the Shivelutch, alt. 10,548 ft. In the Kurile islands, at least ten active volcanoes are known to exist. In the Japan islands there are above twenty; and many of the islands in the strait of Corea are actively volcanic. On the same parallel with some of these volcanoes, but at a great distance from the sea, are several volcanic summits which are only obscurely known. Near the middle of the Tienshan range, or the Celestial mountains, in about N lat. 42° 35', E long. 80°, rises the Peshan or Hoshan, which is said to emit fire and smoke uninterruptedly; and to the E of this mountain, the whole northern slope of the Tienshan presents volcanic phenomena. The solfatara of Urumtsi, in N lat. 46° E long. 84°, is 5 leagues in circumf.; and 200 m. N of Urumtsi, on the banks of the Khobak, is a mountain whose sides are hot, and covered with ammoniacal salts. There is also an insulated conical mountain on Lake Alakul which has been in a state of ignition within historical times. It would thus appear that the valley between the Altai and the Tienshan is the chief seat of volcanic action in Central A.; and this action may be traced across the continent westwards as far as the southern extremity of the Caspian sea, where the Elburz range presents the volcanic peak of Demavend, alt. 14,000 ft. But it is in the chain of islands which skirt the western coast of A., and are prolonged to high southern latitudes, that volcanic action is most formidable. In the island of Java there are no fewer than 38 large volcanic peaks, arranged in a line nearly E and W, affording numerous and unequivocal indications of former activity, and some of which have occasioned great ravages even within the present century. The highest and the most remarkable of these are the Semero in S lat. 8° 08', E long. 113° 09', alt. 13,000 ft.; the Slamat in N lat. 7° 19', E long. 109° 10', alt. 11,329 ft.; Merapi in N lat. 7° 35', E long. 110° 40', alt. 9,708 ft., which was in a state of activity in 1822; Sumbing, alt. 11,028 ft., and Lavu, alt. 10,727 ft. In 1772 one of the largest of the volcanic mountains of Java fell in, and destroyed 40 villages and 2,957 persons, on a tract of ground 15 m. in length and 6 in breadth. From Java the volcanic line continues E through Sumbava, Flores, and Timor. On the Arabian side of the Red sea, volcanic phenomena are numerous; and they have been observed in the mouth of the Persian gulf. Syria and Palestine have been often devastated by earthquakes; and on either side of the sea of Marmora, from the Dardanelles to Constantinople, volcanic indications have been detected. Steppes.] Although A. contains few of those low plains which the ocean seems to have covered at a very remote period, yet we find in this continent many immense tracts of desert and steppe-ground, some of which appear to be vast platforms elevated on the tops of mountains. The Kirghissian steppes extend between the Sungarian chain and the Ural; the Wolgai-Calmuck steppes, from Ural to the Wolga; the Baraba, between the Irtisch and the Obi; the steppes of Isett and Ischim stretch between the Ural chain and the Tobol river, and between the Tobol and the Irtisch. A vast tract of flat marshy country seems to border on the Icy sea. - The Shamo, or Kobi, is a great desert of rock and sand, incapable of culture, or even of supporting vegetation. This name is sometimes given to all the Chinese portion of the deserts and steppes of the great central plateau; but, in strict geography, it should be applied only to the eastern portion of this vast plain, or the country which stretches from the mountains of Altai and Khanggai, to the mountains of Siolki, between 39° and 48° N lat. and 94° and 114° E long. The western portion of this desert is called Chachin. Here winter holds reign for a long period of the year. In the eastern part there occur a few oases which are watered by streams, and afford pasturage to troops of small Mongolian horses; but there is no permanently habitable station throughout the whole of this vast region; the few names which appear in some maps merely indicate the stations at which caravans halt in their route to Mongolia, or the situation of single huts which are occasionally occupied by Chinese posts and carriers. -The desert of Kharesm, or Kharism stretches between the river Sihon and the Caspian sea. It has the desert of Khiva on the NW; the desert of Karakum on the NE; and the Turkoman deserts on the W; and belongs wholly to Independent Tartary. In some parts it presents herbaceous steppes, in which the numerous flocks of the Turkomans feed; in other quarters, nothing is to be seen but ragged flinty rocks; towards the S, between the Sihon and the Tedjin, are vast sandy plains to which the appellation of the desert of Kharesm more properly applies .- The great desert of Hindostan, to the W of the Indus, is 490 m. in length, and 360 m. in breadth. A few oases likewise occur here; and some patches of surface sprinkled with mimosas and grass.—The sandy desert of Iran runs along the western boundary of Afghanistan, to the foot of the Paropamisus on the N, and the Mekran on the S.—The Arabian deserts occupy nearly the whole of the Arabian peninsula. Seas and Coasts.] The maritime coast-line of A. may be reckoned in round numbers at 33,000 m., while that of America is 31,000. We will commence our survey of the seas surrounding this continent, with the Arctic ocean or Northern Icy sea. The Arctic coasts. The Arctic ocean washes the northern coasts of Europe, Asia, and America. To the SE of Nova Zembla, the Asiatic coast-line of this sea commences with the gulf of Kara. Proceeding eastward we observe the gulfs of Obi and Jenisei; which are succeeded by the bay of Taimurskaja, the gulf of Moigolotskaja, with the islands of Kotelnoi, Fadefskoi, and Atrikanskoi, and the gulf of Tschaunskaja, which lies between the Petsharoi and Shalatskoinoso ridges. In summer, says Baron Wrangel, this sea is covered with numerous fragments of ice, which, impelled by the winds and currents, travel about in all directions. There are places where, at little depth, and not far from the currents of the great rivers which throw themselves into this sea, the ice remains immoveable; and there are even certain years in which it does not leave the shores of the continent. Thick mists here obscure the atmosphere, and prevent the action of the sun upon the ice, of which a part only disappears, not from the effect of the heat, but from that of the violent shocks which the masses experience, and which cause them to break against each other: the other part yields to the impetuosity of the currents which carry the masses into more temperate seas. The enormous quantity of floating ice which the large rivers yearly add to that with which the seas are blocked up, greatly surpassing that of which they are freed during summer, the total mass of ice probably increases each year. This opinion seems to be confirmed by the inhabitants of the North cape, from whose observations it would appear that for a very long time back, with the exception of 1820, the sea was never clear of ice; while formerly it was always clear during summer. In winter, the Icy sea presents, on a small scale, an aspect similar to that of the northern part of Siberia. Upon the sea, as upon the land, are seen chains of ice-mountains, with valleys and plains, on which the snow, acquiring the solidity of ice, assumes the form of hillocks resembling waves. The crevices and empty spaces — called polini — which exist between the ice, here take the place of lakes, rivers, and marshes. The inhabitants of the northern parts of Siberia give the general name of torossi to those masses of ice with which the frozen surface of the sea is covered during winter. They are divided into two classes,—the recently broken and the old torossi. The new torossi are the most dangerous for sledges drawn by dogs; the old ones, by the consistence which they acquire, may at the end of a number of years attain the height of 150 ft. At about 25 versts to the N of the island Kotelnoi, the sea is never frozen even during winter. In 1810, M. Hedenstroem penetrated to a polinia, situated 70 versts to the E of New Siberia. Lieutenant Anjou, who went round the northern coast of Kotelnoi and New Siberia, remarked in the sea to the NW of the island, a change of current, which he discovered to come from the flux and reflux. In 1764, Leontief, directing himself to the N of the isle of Four Columns, was stopped at about 40 versts by thin ice which, when it extends to a great distance, is the almost certain indication of a large polinia. M. Wrangel met with this thin ice 79 versts to the NNE of the same island, and, in 1810, was stopped by the polini at 118 versts from the continent, to the NNE of the small rock of Baranof. M. Hedenstroem found, he says, at 250 versts from the coast, a large open crevice in which he observed a strong current from ESE; but, according to Wrangel, this distance is exaggerated. If the Tshutki may be credited, the polinia that is met with at 25 versts from the North cape, extends much farther than that which exists before Yakan. According to the same people, the sea has its current eastward in summer, and westward in autumn; but it may be supposed that in this matter the Russian interpreter was deceived, for a singular exception would result from this fact. M. Wrangel's map indicates that this sea is deeper to the E than to the N. At 200 versts to the N of the small rock of Baranof, the depth is only 12 sageries 2 feet; while it was found to be 17 sageries at the distance of 10 m. from the great rock of Baranof, by the crew of the Yassuschneia, in 1787. The opinion | the other by the straits of Babelmandel. On the that the sea is retiring from the northern coasts of Siberia, is common among the inhabitants of these coasts. It seems chiefly founded upon the observation which they have made that, at 50 versts from the sea, on shallows which the waters no longer reach, there is found a quantity of decayed trees. The island of Diomede, marked to the E of Cape Saint on Schelavurof's map, and which formed a narrow sound with the mainland through which that navigator sailed, no longer exists, from which it has been supposed that the strait has been filled up.— The extremely salt taste of the internal layers of the ice in the Icy sea, proves in what immense quantity salt occurs in it.—The refraction of the land has a great action upon the Icy sea: the ice-mountains and the torossi under it assume new forms, and seem as if suspended in the air. The effect of this refraction would be to present the most remote objects to the view; but it is neutralized by that which the uniform and deceitful whiteness, which covers all these countries, produces upon the eyes. The coasts of the Pacific.] The Pacific, or Southern ocean, is connected with the Arctic ocean by Behring's straits, and stretches along the whole eastern coasts of Asia and New Holland. From the southern extremity of Van Diemen's Land to the Antarctic polar circle, it is arbitrarily divided from the Indian ocean by the line which indicates the 147th eastern meridian.—The sea of Kamtschatka, the most northern part of this great ocean, lies between the Asiatic and American continents; and forms three great gulfs belonging to Asia, viz. the gulf of Anadir, betwixt Tschuktschoi Noss and St. Thaddeus; the gulf of Olutorskaja to the S of Anadir; and the gulf of Kamtschatka to the W of the peninsula of that name.—Another Asiatic division of the Pacific ocean is the sea of Okhotsk, which lies between the eastern coast of Siberia, the great island of Tchoka, the island of Jesso, the Kuriles, and the peninsula of Kamtschatka. That portion of it which extends between Siberia and Kamtschatka is sometimes called the sea of Penshina.—The sea of Japan washes the coasts of Mandshuria, the Corean peninsula, Japan, Jesso, and Tchoka. The straits of Perouse connect it with the sea of Okhotsk; the straits of Matsmai with the main ocean; and the straits of Corea with the Whang-hai or Yellow sea. -The Yellow sea washes the shores of Mandshuria, China, Corea, the Formosa islands, and the Loo-Choo group. The gulf of Petscheli is formed in its NW part. Were we to pursue the geography of this great ocean any farther in this quarter, we should next encounter those groups of islands which some geographers have divided into the three great classes of the Asiatic Archipelago, Australasia, and Polynesia. Coasts of the Indian ocean.] The limits of the Indian ocean may be regarded as traced by Asia on the N; by the islands of Sunda, New Holland, and the Pacific on the E; by the Antarctic ocean on the S; and by Africa and the Atlantic on the W,—the cape of Good Hope, and the meridian of the Cape, marking its western limits, and the 30th parallel its extreme northern limits. It is divided into two principal parts, the Indian equinoctial ocean to the N of the tropic of Capricorn,—and the Indian southern ocean to the S of the same tropic. The former is the more considerable division. The Asiatic continent projects two vast peninsulas into this ocean,those of Arabia and Hindostan, which are separated from each other by the sea of Oman or the Arabian sea. The former peninsula is determined on the NE and SW by two long and narrow seas,—the Persian gulf, and the Arabian gulf; the one communicating with the Indian ocean by the straits of Ormus, western coast of Hindostan we perceive the gulfs of Cutch and Cambay, which form the peninsula of Guzerat. SE from this peninsula are the gulf of Manaar and the straits of Palk, running between the island of Ceylon and the continent. Between Hindostan and Indo-China is the great gulf of Bengal, forming the gulf of Martaban on the E, and enclosing the Andaman and Nicobar group. To the SE of this large gulf are the straits of Malacca, lying between the peninsula of Malacca and the isle of Sumatra, and communicating with the Chinese sea. The latter sea washes the southern coasts of China, the eastern coasts of Lower India, the shores of Borneo, the Philippines, the Babuyan and Bashi groups and Pelan. Its principal gulfs are those of Tonquin, Siam, and Mindoro. The Mediterranean coast.] The eastern part of the Mediterranean washes the Syrian, Karamanian, and Anadolian shores of A. From El-Arisch to the head of its extreme eastern indentation of this continent, the gulf of Iskenderun, the general direction of the coast of this sea is NE; at the head of that gulf, it assumes a general WNW direction; and forms in succession the gulfs of Adalia, Makri, Syme, and Kos; and in its beautiful arm, the Ægean, or Archipelago, the gulfs of Mandelyah, Scala Nova, Smyrna, and Adramytti; beyond which the Dardanelles or Hellespont running NE, betwixt the meridians of 26° and 27° E, a distance of 50 m., conduct into the Propontis or Sea of Marmora, which communicates with the Black sea by the Bosphorus or straits of Constantinople, and the extreme length of which, from strait to strait, in an ENE direction, is 110 geographical miles. The Euxine. The Euxine or Black sea belongs in common to Europe and Asia. It washes the Turkish and Caucasian districts of the latter continent, dividing them from the southern provinces of Russia; and extends nearly 700 m. from W to E, between the meridians of 28° and 41° 30' E. Inland seas and lakes.] "The continent of Asia," says Malte Brun, "forming a considerable body of land, and but little intersected by seas, ought naturally to contain in its interior great accumulations of water;" and in fact it does contain, as we have already seen, a considerable number of inland collections of water, amongst which is the largest lake in the world, the Caspian sea, having an area of 160,000 sq. m. To the E of the Caspian, and about onefourth of its size, is the sea of Aral, which is situated in the Karismian steppes, and divides the country of the Karakalpack hordes from the districts inhabited by the Aralians and Turkomans. It has no visible outlet, although it receives the contributions of two great rivers, viz., the Sirr or Sihon, and the Amu- Daria or Oxus on the S. The other great lakes of A. are: the lake of Urumiyah in the province of Azerdbijan; the lake of Erivan; the lakes of Shiraz and Bakhtigan in Farsistan; the Dead sea in the pashalik of Damascus; the lake Van in the pashalik of Van; the Zurrah; the Kao-Yeou-Hou and the Hong- Ise-Hou in the Chinese prov. of Kiangnan; the Tong- Ting-Hou in the centre of China; the Terkiri in Tibet; the Koko-Nor on the borders of Tibet; the Lop-Nor in Little Bucharia; the Kosogol in Mongolia; the Zaisan in Mongolia; the Argun-dalai in Mandshuria; the Balkash or Palkati-Nor in Sungaria; the Tchani and the Piasinskoe in the Russian gov. of Tomsk; and the large Baikal in the gov. of Irkutsk. The disagreement of writers in assigning the dimensions of the Asiatic lakes argues a very imperfect acquaintance with the interior of this continent. In general, the lakes of A. are distinguished by their saline, brackish, or sulphureous waters; and many of them, in the western and central parts of the continent have no outlet. The two peninsulas of India have no remarkable lakes. Rivers.] Many magnificent rivers belong to this continent. The Hoang-Ho, rising in the mountains of Tibet, after a winding course of 2,280 m., discharges its immense stream into the Chinese sea. The Kiang-Ku or Yang-Tse-Kiang, rises near the sources of the Hoang-Ho, and after washing the walls of Nankin, falls into the Chinese sea about 100 m. to the S of the former. These two rivers are considered as the longest on the face of the globe. The winding course of the latter is estimated at 2,880 m. They equal the famous river of the Amazons in South America; and the majestic course of the Ganges is not half the length.—The Ob or Obi, the largest river of the Russian empire, issues from the lake of Altyn, and after traversing the frozen regions of Siberia in a course of 1,276 m. direct distance, or 2,320 including its windings, discharges itself into the Arctic ocean.—The Jenisei, rising near the foot of the Altai mountains, waters the country of the Tunguse Tartars, and falls into the Icy sea, after a course of 1,228 m. direct distance.—The Lena, which takes its rise near the sea or lake of Baikal, after running in a north-eastern direction about 2,400 m., discharges itself into the Frozen sea.—The Amur, which is deservedly classed amongst the largest rivers, takes its rise near the Yablonnoi mountains, where it is first known by the names of Kerlon and Argun; and, after pursuing an easterly course of about 1,200 m. in direct distance, but nearly double that length measured along its windings, through Chinese Tartary, where it is augmented by many tributary streams, the most considerable of which is the Sungari, empties itself into the sea of Kamtschatka. — The celebrated and sacred stream of the Ganges, fertilizes the vast plains of India, and dividing itself into two branches, falls into the gulf of Bengal. It receives so many important tributary streams, that its magnitude exceeds what might have been expected from the comparative length of its course, which is estimated at about 1,680 m., or 824 m. in direct distance.—The Brahmaputra, or, as it is called by the Tibetans, the Sampu, is the noblest tributary stream of the Ganges. It takes its rise in the same lofty mountains of Tibet which give source to the Ganges; and after proceeding 1,400 m. in a southern direction, loses itself in the estuary of the Ganges. This noble river flows 400 m. through the British territory; and for the last 60 m., before its junction with the Ganges, it is from 4 to 5 m. in breadth. The Indus, which waters the western parts of India, as the Ganges does the eastern, after a course which is estimated at 1,000 m., direct distance, discharges itself by many mouths into the Indian sea.— The Euphrates, the principal river in the SW of Asia, takes its rise in the mountains of Armenia, and chiefly pursues a SW direction to Samisat, where it would flow into the Mediterranean, unless prevented by a high ridge of mountains. At this point it assumes a SE direction, and after receiving the Tigris, falls by two or three mouths into the gulf of Persia. Its course is estimated at about 1,500 m.—The rivers now enumerated, with many others, and some of them almost equally important, are minutely described under their respective articles. We may here remark one feature common to most of the Asiatic rivers, which descend towards the south. Taking their rise in the high central region, and flowing through countries subject to periodical rains, by carrying down immense quantities of mud and earth, they have formed vast fertile plains near the sea coasts, similar in their nature to the Delta of Lower Egypt. Accordingly, in the S of Asia, from Persia to China, near the mouth of the Indus, the Ganges, and other rivers, immense tracts of level country are found, which are periodically overflowed; and which, aided by the warmth of the climate, exhibit a degree of fertility in all the productions of the vegetable world, of which, in our temperate and colder regions, we have little conception. Malte Brun gives the following estimate of the proportional volumes, or, to speak more exactly, of the surfaces of the running waters of this part of the world. Instead of a channel 150 m. wide,—as we find in the Plata and Amazon,—the widest rivers of Asia seldom exceed 4 m. or 5 m.; and their depth likewise, in general, is not great. Climate.] A., extending from the polar circle to the neighbourhood of the equator, must necessarily exhibit a great variety of temperature in its different regions: in no part, however, is the climate so intolerably hot as in the tropical desert of the African continent. It may be said, generally, that in the south-western part, even including Arabia, which is within the tropic, it is temperate; but in the southeastern great heat prevails; while, throughout the northern half of this continent, excessive cold predominates. Hassel describes the Asiatic climate under four zones or belts: The 1st of these includes all the country lying to the N of the 62d parallel; in which the rivers continue frozen from September to June. The 2d extends between the 50th and 62d parallels, including Southern Siberia, the kingdom of Kasan, a great part of the kingdom of Astrakhan, the half of the Kirghissian steppes, the northern half of Mongolia and Mandshuria, the island of Tchoka, the Aleutian islands, and the Kuriles; the rivers are here frozen from the end of October to the end of May. The 3d zone comprehends the countries between the 35th and 50th parallels; or a great part of the central plateau, the remaining portion of Mongolia and Mandshuria, Corea, Northern China, Little Bucharia, the Aral and Caspian territories, the Caucasian districts, the southern part of Astrakhan, the whole of Anatolia, Armenia, a part of Persia and Afghanistan, and a part of Japan. In this zone, however, the climate is greatly modified by the physical conformation of the country: the sudden change of level produces powerful effects where latitude does not come into operation. The temperature is severely cold, for example, on plateaus whose elevation exceeds 600 toises or 3,800 ft. above the level of the sea; while in the terraces and valleys we find an agreeable climate, under which rice, olives, and cotton, are reared. In the 4th zone, comprising all the countries of Asia lying to the S of the 35th parallel, that is, the southern part of Asiatic Turkey, Arabia, the S of Persia and Afghanistan, the two peninsulas of India and the neighbouring islands, and the S of China and of Japan, there are only two seasons known. From April to November the sun is nearly perpendicular, and constant rains deluge one country, while another is parched by unyielding drought. During the rest of the year the sky is serene. Vegetation is uncommonly luxuriant in this zone.-Malte Brun ascribes the long duration of the same wind which we here observe in countries at a distance from the tropics, to the absence of gulfs and seas whose exhalations and currents might alter the nature of the wind or change its direction. "The chilling winds of Siberia," he says, "ascend even to the summits of the centre of the great plateau; and, if sufficiently elevated to pass the first chains, they may extend to the heights of Tibet. The wind from the E, charged with fogs, covers at once all the lower parts of China; but as we get farther into the temperate zone, all regularity in the united action of the sea and the atmosphere gradually ceases. Thus, at Japan, cold and heat,—storms and calms,—succeed each other almost as rapidly as in Great Britain. China, either on account of the great humidity of the sea-breezes, or the dryness of those which have passed over the land, is liable to these variations in a less sensible manner than Holland. In short, in passing through the temperate Oriental countries, the seasons always become more constant but colder in proportion as we approach the centre. Nearly the same changes are perceptible in going from the W to the E of Europe. In Northern A. there is another feature which strikes us as very remarkable, comparing that region with parts of Europe under the same latitudes. The cold of Northern A. always increases as we proceed towards the E; and this augmentation is so great, that, upon the coasts of Tartary, situated under the same latitudes as France, the winter commences in the month of September. Several causes doubtless combine to produce this phenomenon. In the first place, there rise between Corea and the countries upon the river Amur, vast mountains covered with glaciers; a second, and still greater mass of mountains separates the Amur from the Lena: all the coasts of the NE are also extremely steep; and, we may add, that the seas which surround these frozen countries are almost always covered with thick and cold fogs, which intercept the rays of the sun. A third cause may be found in the absolute want of inhabitants, and consequently of cultivation. In Eastern Siberia, according to official reports, there is scarcely 1 individual to 7 sq. m. We must consider the mass of air superincumbent on a continent as a whole, the general modification of which depends on all the partial modifications. If a continent extend far into the torrid zone, the mass of warm air re-acts upon the temperate mass, and communicates to it a part of its caloric, and, dilating, forces it towards the north, and thus confines the limits of the cold: so that the countries toward the poles do not simply become cold in the direct ratio of their latitudes. This increase of cold also observes an inverse ratio to the extent of heated land contiguous to them on the S. Such is the reason why the neighbourhood of the immense mass of heated surface in Africa renders the temperature of Arabia, of Syria, and of Mesopotamia, hotter than it should otherwise be. In the winter season, the cold of North America is very piercing in the environs of the tropic. That portion of this continent which extends to the S of the tropic of Cancer, is small in comparison with the remainder: hence, there is no mass of warm air to re-act on the temperate and cold, so that the action of the cold mass receives no counterbalance. If we examine the map of A., we shall see the form of that continent contracting in breadth from China to Behring's straits, at which part the climate is no longer warm. The air in these countries, naturally cold, is rendered still more so by the influence of the frozen sea: the great Pacific ocean is not adequate to counterbalance its effects, being itself cooled by the number of icebergs which enter it through Behring's straits. These icebergs are often stopped between the Aleutian islands and Andrinof, and occasion the cold fogs with which that part of the sea is covered. They are afterwards carried by the general current of the ocean from E to W, that is, from America to Asia, where they accumulate in the gulfs. This unchangeableness of physical circumstances,— 2 A those climates which no industry can sensibly amehorate,—those invariable returns of the seasons,— that certain repetition of the same mode of cultivation, and consequently of the same mode of living, must have an influence on the moral character of the Asiatics, as well in modifying their nervous and muscular system, as in toning their imagination to the unvaried return of the same sensations." Vegetable kingdom.] The productions of A., under all the three kingdoms of Nature, are very rich and varied. The productions of the earth, under equally favourable circumstances with respect to soil and irrigation, will vary nearly as the climate: in A., therefore, we find the members of the vegetable kingdom exceedingly numerous. The flora of A. is characterised by the greatest richness and variety, corresponding to the extreme diversity of climateric influences which its different regions exhibit. In some localities, such as on the Himalaya, the entire series of climateric and vegetable zones, from the polar to the equatorial, is remarkably developed under the same parallel; and in others their gradations, under the operation of more widely extending, or otherwise dissimilar circumstances, are not always very clearly defined. Generally, however, the flora of the different regions of A. is of a sufficiently homogeneous character to admit of the following local distribution, viz.: 1st, The Siberian region, comprehending the vast extent stretching from the sea of Kamtschatka on the E, to the Ural mountains on the W; and from the shores of the Frozen ocean to the confines of China, properly so called, and the mountains bordering on the N of Tibet. The more westerly section of this region, extending from the borders of Russia in Europe to the Jenisei, presents a floral physiology closely allied to the European. To the E of that river, the more strictly indigenous vegetation of the Asiatic continent begins to present itself. The explorations of botanical research in the vast region of Siberia have hitherto however extended little beyond the banks of the Jaïk, Irtish, Obi, and Selenga, the environs of Lake Baikal, and a few other districts that commerce has thrown open to research. The greater number of the genera which characterise the flora of Siberia belong to the families of the umbelliferæ, rosaceæ, synanthereæ, gentianeæ, gramineæ, cyperaceæ, cruciferæ, leguminosæ, and ranunculaceæ. Of the first named genus the most remarkable species are those of the ligusticum and selinum which are found on the banks of the Jaïk and Lena. The spiræa genus of the rosaceous tribe is peculiar to the Siberian region of Asia. Asters, worm-wood, gentians, pediculaires, dauphinelles, carices, &c., abound to the E of the Jenissei. Of the umbelliferæ, the astragalus genus, indigenous to Siberia, is distributed in multitudes of species over the entire extent of that vast region, and represents a numerous class remarkable for the modifications of which it is susceptible, and for the affinities which it consequently elicits with the vegetation of the adjacent districts of NW America, Western China, the Caucasus, and Southern Russia. The 2d great botanical region of A., distinguished as the Sinico- Japan, includes the whole of the kingdom of Annam, Cochin- China, and all the islands of the Chinese sea, dependant upon Japan. Until the publication of the 'Flora Japonica,' near the close of the last century, by the indefatigable Dr. Thunberg, and more recently, the 'Flora Cochinchinensis,' by the Portuguese missionary Loureiro, little was known of the vegetation of any portion of this vast region. Of the botany of China we are still to a great extent destitute of any precise information, and the general cultivation of its soil renders the knowledge of its indigenous productions extremely difficult of attainment; but considerable analogical evidence exists, that they bear a close affinity to that of Japan, with fewer, perhaps, of the characteristic features of the Indian flora, but exhibiting in greater abundance those of the Siberian or European. Thus the environs of Pekin exhibit many plants common to those of Paris. The productions belonging to this kingdom most remarkable either for beauty or utility are to be met with more commonly in the southern provinces. Of these the principal are the hortensia, the Hibiscus Sinensis, the splendid aster or reine-marguerite, the beautiful primrose, known in Europe as the Primula sinensis, and the magnificent liliac coloured Glycine Sinensis. But of all the herbaceæ of China the most important as an article of luxury and of commercial value is the tea-plant (Thea viridis). Of this plant there exists but one botanical species, the different qualities of its produce arising from different methods of culture and preparation; and from the admixture of layers of the Olea fragrans and Camellia sasanqua which abound in the tea districts. The Illicium anisatum is also indigenous to this region. -The flora of the Japan isles is remarkable for the closeness of its analogical affinity to the vegetation both of Europe and India. The veronica, iris, carex, campanula, chenopodium, allium, juncus, euphorbia, &c., represent the former; while the canna, amomum, Justicia, celastrus, carissa, dioscorea, diospyros, paullinia, and laurus, represent the flora of the tropical regions of India. The most remarkable of the indigenous productions are the Olea fragrans, the Aukuba Japonica, the sweetly odorous Gardenia florida, the Rhus vernix or varnish-tree, the Aralia cordata and the elegant pentaphylla and japonica. Of the liliaceæ family are the Amaryllis Sarninsis, Lilium Japonicum, Hemerocallis Japonica, and cordata, the much admired camellia, the Daphne odora, the sophora, pyrus, mespilus and several spiræa, of which the corchorus or Spiræa Japonica is now commonly naturalized in Europe. region, with the exception of those lying between the Caspian and Black seas adjacent to the ranges of the Caucasus and Taurus, and of the low plains in the vicinity of the sea, the arenaceous soil of which is impregnated with salt, presents a peculiar vegetation approximating to that of eastern and southern Europe. The botanical productions of the Mediterranean coasts of Asia Minor are closely allied to those of Greece, Italy, and Egypt. Syria. and some other districts, comprising great diversity of soil, contain a corresponding richness of specific variety. Of these varieties the most remarkable is the celebrated cedar of Lebanon—the Pinus cedrus of Linnæus, and the Cedrus excelsa of more modern botanists -now however rapidly becoming obsolete. Other indigenous plants of this section of Asia—especially those of commercial value, such as the astragalus, which affords the adracanth gum— are on the contrary becoming greatly multiplied. Little has hitherto been ascertained with respect to the vegetation of Persia. It appears however, from the hortus siccus of Belanger and some other travellers, that it retains some of the features of the flora of Asia Minor, without however any admixture of the European; while it approximates in several of its genera to the botanical productions of Hindostan. The liliac, cyclamen, several species of the carnation, and rose, &c., are indigenous to this region. The vegetation of the districts of Arabia, adjacent to the Red sea-the only portion of that peninsula which has hitherto been successfully explored by botanists—is nearly allied to that of Egypt; and, with the exception of the arid district of Suez, is characterised by extreme luxuriance and beauty. In the environs of Tora village situated at the base of Mount Sinai-fruit-trees of various species are cultivated in abundance; and plantations of palms are occasionally to be met with. In the rich and diversified province of Yemen, the flora of Arabia assumes a more eastern character. exhibiting in its sandy districts the Corypha umbraculifera, or fan-leaved palm of the Eastern Indies, &c. The coffee-plant, Coffae Arabica, grows indigenously in the Jebel-Essuad; but is generally cultivated throughout Arabia. The tract of country between Jibbla and Taas abounds with a small species of arborescent euphorbium, and contains individuals of the Mimosa Nilotica, and several other species of the same class common to Africa. The much famed aromatic plants, and the various species of grain which belong to the Arabian peninsula, are too numerous to admit of any attempt at enumeration in the present general description. The 4th, and superlatively rich botanical region of the Asiatic continent is that of India. Little was known of the peculiar productions of this vast region until the publication of the 'Flora Indica' by Burmann, and the more recent work of the same name published under the united auspices of Drs. Hamilton, Carey, and Wallich. For the extent and accuracy of acquaintance which we now possess with the vegetation of the Indian peninsula, we are chiefly indebted to the 'Planta Asiatica rariores' of Dr. Wallich. The flora of the Himalaya chain, which skirts the northern frontier of Hindostan, presents a close affinity to that of more northerly latitudes; that of Nepal for instance exhibits many generaof which one of the most remarkable is the horse-chestnut, æsculus hippocastanum-generally known in Europe. But here, in proportion to the vicinity to the equator, vegetation assumes the characteristics of that region; and in the elevated mountain plateaux it rivals in luxuriance the richest portions of the New world. In the multiplicity of its productions it includes numerous species of the canneæ family, such as the canna, amomum, costus, zingiber, maranta, curcuma, &c. The black pepper (Piper nigrum), and the betel (Piper betel), grow in great quantities on the Malabar coast. Varieties of the gramineæ abound throughout the peninsula. Some of the monocotyledones, remarkable for their elegance, such as the Crinum Asiaticum, Polyanthes tuberosa, Methonica superba, Flagellaria Indica, several species of the amaryllis, the pancratium, aloe, &c., are peculiar to the warmer regions of India. The greater number of the laurineæ belong to the same localities. Of the latter the Laurus cinnamomum, L. camphora, L. malabathrum, &c., form, especially in the island of Ceylon, forests of great extent. The nutmeg-tree (Myristica officinalis), clove-tree (Caryophyllus aromaticus), the Eugenia jambos, are occasionally, although not commonly, to be met with in the Indian peninsula. The principal leguminosæ are the well known tamarind (Tamarindus Indica), the Cæsalpinia sappan, Guilandina bonduc, Moringa oleifera, several kinds of cassia, &c. The fragrant Daphne Indica, the mangove (Mangifera Indica), the guave (Psidium pomiferum), the Durio zibethinus, and the Garcinia mangostana, form also remarkable specimens of the flora of this great region. The prevailing fruit-trees, in the more cultivated districts. are the mangifera, citrus, artocarpus, eugenia, elate, and borassus. The forests consist principally of the rhizophora, ægiceras, avicennia, sonneratia, and heritiera genera, especially the latter. The teak, bamboo, and palm-tree, are peculiarly characteristic of Asiatic forest-timber; a great variety of very fine woods for cabinetpurposes are also procured on this continent. Animal kingdom.] The distribution of the animal kingdom in A. corresponds generally with the great geognostic characteristics of the continent; and may consequently be divided into the three great zones which are traced by the two mountain-systems which run, in lines parallel to the equator, across the entire extent of the continent. The central zone is distinguished from the two lateral zones by the comparatively few rivers of any extent which intersect its surface; and by the prevalence of those immense tracts of sandy and often saline wastes, commonly called steppes. The most remarkable of the aboriginal animal species of this region are the Bactrian, or two-humped camel; the tarpan or wild The flora of the northern districts of the 3d, or Arabico-Persic | horse; the jaghatai, a species of horse whose fleetness obtains for it from the Monguls an appellation signifying 'the sun's courser;' several varieties of oxen, the peculiarly flowing tails of which form a frequent decoration in the military insignia of the East; two species of antelopes,-the zeren or yellow buck, an animal of extraordinary swiftness,—and the saiga. Panthers, and the manul, the original of the Angora cat, are not uncommon in the more western districts. The Indian tiger is occasionally to be met with in Siberia, even as far north as the sources of the Obi; and it is still found in considerable numbers in the forests of Mazanderan and Azerdbijan. It likewise haunts the banks of the Keroulun, and the Orkhon, as far as the Altai; and is found over the entire kingdom of China. Various species of dogs, wolves, jackals, antelopes, asses, and wild horses, are extremely numerous. The musk-cat is common on all the mountain-ranges which surround this zone. The Altai is frequented also by the argali or Siberian sheep; the southern mountains by the egagre, or wild-goat; and the Caucasus by a peculiar species of goat, and by the chamois. The western extension of this region, which terminates in Arabia, is inhabited by numerous species of antelope, by the blue-goat, the algazel, the gazelle, and the one-humped camel or dromedary, now very extensively naturalized in other countries. The lions, panthers, caracals, and other species of cats, the jackals, antelopes, and monkeys, which frequent the peninsula of Arabia and Persia, evince considerable affinity to those of the African continent. The onagre, or wild ass, remarkable for its beauty and fleetness, frequents the mountain districts and plateaux of these countries, and the environs of the Caspian. The banks of the rivers, and the vast forests of the northern zone, abound with rein-deer, elks, wolves, common blue and black foxes, bears, wolverenes, and several species of the martin and squirrel. The shores of the Frozen ocean are inhabited by the polar bear, and abound with seals and various kinds of cetacea. A peculiar species of seal or otter is common both in the Caspian and Lake Baikal. At the Junction of Europe and A., on the coasts of the Black sea, and on the Caucasus, the European and Asiatic forms of animals are mixed, and pass into each other.—The third or southern zone exhibits the greatest exuberance of animal life. The plains of Scinde abound with several peculiar species of the antelope; and the forests of the interior, especially those which cover the acclivities and summits of the Himalaya, contain five or six species of the stag, until recently altogether unknown in Europe. The graceful four-horned chitckara, and immense herds of the blue antelope, are found in Tibet. The beautiful white-spotted axis is common in the forests of Bengal; those of Orissa are frequented by the jungly-gau, the normal representative of the domestic oxen of India. The banks of the Ganges are infested by the blackstriated tiger; and the muddy shores of the seas, and of all the lakes and rivers of the Indian continent abound with the black shaggy headed buffalo. The forest-tracts stretching between the Indus and Ganges contain immense numbers of squirrels, peacocks, pheasants, and jungle-cocks. Several species of bears inhabit the forests of the Ghauts and of the mountains of Mysore. The Indian elephant and the one-horned rhinoceros are common in the forest solitudes of the same district; but it is only in Transgangetic India that these quadrupeds appear to attain their full dimensions. The latter region possesses also the bicoloured tapir of Malacca and China, the ourang-outang, and various species of apes.—The rivers of A. abound with fish. In the Ganges alone 250 species have been described by Hamilton. The delta of Bengal is infested by several species of serpents, of which however one only is venomous.—The bird-tribes of the Asiatic continent are extremely rich and diversified. Vultures of various species, eagles, falcons, buzzards, and screech-owls, are common on the banks of the Indus. Parrots of every variety of plumage are to be found throughout the entire extent of the southern continent, and in all the adjacent islands. The beautiful loris, the delicately white cockatoo, and the enamelled pheasants, are common in all the warmer regions of A. India is the native country of the peacock, the domestic cock and hen, and a great proportion of the gallinaceous tribes, which are generally extremely beautiful. The heath-cock of Pegu, the Nepal pheasant, the Impayan pheasant, the gold and silver pheasants of China, the Argus pheasant of Borneo, and many others of this tribe of birds, are exquisitely beautiful. All the common European species of birds occur on this continent. The ostrich is probably no longer to be found in Asia; but its congener, the cassowary, inhabits the islands of the Indian archipelago.—The reptilia of Asia include tortoises, crocodiles, boa-constrictors, and numerous species of the sauria, ophidia, and batrachia orders. All the seas which surround the Asiatic continent abound with fish, which are nearly assimilated to those of the continent. The cetacea are principally found along the northern coasts. Various species of dolphins inhabit the tropical seas; and the Halicore or dugong is found off the coasts of the large islands in the Indian ocean. The most celebrated of the fresh water fish is the <i>gurami</i>.—Of the insect tribes the butterflies, cigalæ, and libellulæ, present the richest varieties. The locust appears occasionally in the NW regions of India, and it often invades in countless myriads the plains of Arabia and Syria. The pearl-oyster constitutes the most important species of the mollusca class. Mineral kingdom.] Although A. is generally considered as less rich in metals than the other great continents, so mountainous a continent cannot be deficient in minerals, however little its mineral wealth may have been explored hitherto. In the precious gems it is peculiarly rich. Rock-crystals and amethysts are found in many quarters; the common and rose quartz in the Altai mountains; chalcedony in Mongolia; cat's-eye in Ceylon and its items, appears to us to be rather an over-estimate. Malabar; obsidian in the vicinity of some of the volcanic mountains; pyrope, or fire-stone, in Mount Carmel; jasper, heliotrope, and topaz, in the Ural; aquamarine between Nertschinsk and the Baikal; zeolites in Siberia; zircon and hyacinth in Ceylon; the onyx in Persia; kaolin or porcelain earth in China and Japan. The diamonds of Hindostan, and the pearls of Bahrein and Manaar are well-known; the diamond is also found in Ceylon, and in Asiatic Russia; and rubies and turquoises are brought from the same regions. Rock-salt, soda, rock-butter, saltpetre, and naphtha, are procured from the soil in various places. The rivers of A. Minor washed down fragments of gold in ancient times, and silver-mines were wrought on Mount Sipylus within the last century. The gold of Arabia is frequently mentioned in scripture; and gold-dust is still collected from the streams of Kabul and Kandahar. This precious metal is also found in the Birman empire, in China, in Japan, in Siam, in Malacca, and in the Ural chain in Asiatic Russia. The total produce of gold from the sands of the Ural and Siberia, between the years 1819 and 1846, was 14,335 poods, of 40 lb. Russian, or 36 lb. English each. Silver and copper mines are wrought in the neighbourhood of Tocat; quicksilver and zinnobar in China; malachite in China; and iron, and magnet in Siberia; molybdena near Kolywan; and chromium near Beresof. Tin is found all over the Malay peninsula, but not to the northward of 10°. Lead exists in China, Siam, Japan, Georgia, and Armenia. Population.] Both sacred history and profane have placed the primeval seat of the human race in Asia, and concur in pointing out this region as the quarter whence population has gradually extended over the rest of the earth. While most of the nations inhabiting the continents of Europe, Afrcai, and America have been traced to a foreign origin, the population of A. has, on all hands, been allowed to be wholly primitive and original, if we except perhaps the Tchuktches, a small tribe in the north-east, the Russian colonies in the north, and the well-known European settlements in the south. The number of the human race in this quarter of the world is immense, though below what might be expected in regions so early peopled, and abounding in every production which can contribute to the subsistence or the comfort of mankind. But the feeble and despotic governments of A. have been most unfavourable to the increase of its population; and the amount to which it has attained, in spite of this powerful check, is one of the best proofs of the kindness of nature, and the luxuriance of the soil throughout much of this continent. The pop. of A. has been estimated at above 600,000,000; but this is in a great measure conjecture: the number of inhabitants in its various regions having never been ascertained with any degree of precision. Europeans have but little access to information; and the reports of the natives themselves are not to be trusted. They neither possess accurate knowledge on this subject, nor are they disposed to give a just statement if they did. There is reason to believe, that the accounts which have been received under this head, in many instances greatly exceed the truth. The pop. of China in particular,—which forms the chief article in these accounts, appears, from the report of M. de Guignes, to have been enormously exaggerated, when carried, as it has been, by some to 350,000,000, and even 400,000,000. We are, however, after all, very imperfectly acquainted with the population of this continent. In the 'Weimar Almanack' for 1848 it is estimated at 665,000,000 in 1844. Some British geographers conjecture it may amount to 600,000,000; and Templeman approximates it to nearly the same number. But in Macculloch's Geographical Dictionary, a pop. of only 375,230,000 is assigned to Continental Asia; and 54,370,000 to the islands: making 429,600,000 in all. The 'Echo of Truth,' a Neapolitan Journal published in 1829, estimated it only at 330,000,000; and the Parisian geographers in 1824 at 480,000,000. Hassel estimated it in 1821 at 490,000,000. The Caucasian provinces, Tribes according to colour.] Were we to arrange the population of A. into classes distinguished by colour, we should say that three races of mankind inhabited this continent,—the White, the Yellow, and the Black. The latter are few in number; and we may therefore consider the other two as dividing this part of the world between them. The White race occupy nearly the whole of Western A.; the Yellow race inhabit the rest. In some instances the two races have become blended together, and it would be difficult to say to which race their descendants belong. In the White race we would class all the Caucasian tribes, the pop. of Asiatic Turkey, of Arabia, of Kurdistan, of Persia, of Afghanistan, the Bukharians, Armenians, Georgians, Turkomans, Uzbeks, Kirghissians, all the inhabitants of Hindostan, of Nepal, of Ceylon and the Maldives, and several tribes of Asiatic Russia, such as the Jakoutes, the Voguls, the Permians, the Syrians, the Tchouvaches, the Morduins, and the Ostiaks of the Obi .- The Yellow race would comprehend the Calmucks, the Khalkhas of Central Asia, the Samoiedes, the Lamutes, the Joukagirs, the Tchouktchis and the Koriaks, all of Asiatic Russia; the Mongols and Tungouses who live as nomades in Asiatic Russia and China; the Mandshus, the Coreans, the Japanese, the Chinese, the Anamitains, the Siamese, the Birmans, and the Tibetians.-The Malays seem to be a mixed race of Whites and Yellows.—The Negroes belong to Ceylon, Andaman, and Nicobar. ASIA ISLES, a group of three thickly wooded islets in the Pacific ocean, in N lat 1°, E long. 131° 30'.

ASIAGO, a district and town of Lombardy, in the gov. of Venice, and prov. of Vicenza, bordering on Tyrol. It comprises 7 com., and is known as the Sette Comuni, or 'Seven communities.' A line drawn round their territory would embrace nearly 38 Italian sq. m., with a pop. of about 1,000 to each mile. The country is surrounded by deep, narrow, barren valleys, the greater part of which is uninhabitable. The natural produce of the territory is scanty. Cattle and sheep are the principal exports of this territory; and cabinet-work, turnery ware, and straw-hats form the chief articles of manufacture. The inhabitants of this district are supposed to be the descendants of those remains of the Cimbri, who, having invaded Italy in the year of Rome 640, were defeated by Marius in the Campagna de Verona. They are a hardy race, of warlike disposition, and still speak a German dialect. When subjected by the republic of Venice, they were charged with the defence of the passes from the Tyrol into the Vicentine territory. These passes are 17 in number, 3 of which are within the precinct of the Sette Comuni. The other six comuni are Rozzo, Enego, Foza, Gallia, Roana, and Lusiana.—The principal town, of the same name, stands upon the side of the Montagna d'Asiago, which rises from a horizontal plain nearly 15 m. in extent, surrounded by gentle hills, 36 m. N by W of Vicenza. It has a pop. of 12,000.
ASIKKALA, a town of Russia, in Finland, in the prov. of Helsingfors, 42 m. ENE of Tavastehus, in N lat. 61° 13'.
ASILLO, a town of Upper Peru, on the N shore of the NW limb of Lake Titicaca, on the road from Asangaro to Lampa.

ASIMAGOMY LAKE, a sheet of water in Upper Canada, in N lat. 48° 35', discharging itself into the E end of Lake Superior. It is not far from the sources of the Moose which flows in the opposite direction to James's bay.
ASIMALUNGA, a town of Tuscany, 20 m. NW of Arezzo. Pop. 3,220.

ASINARA, an island of irregular outline, divided from the NW point of Sardinia by a narrow shallow strait. It is 10 m. in length from NE to SW, and about 5 m. in breadth; and is mountainous throughout, rising in Monte Scommunica to an alt. of 1,500 ft. Trabucato point, on its E side, is in N lat. 41° 4' 4", E long. 8° 18' 53". Its only inhabitants are a few shepherds and fishers.
ASINARA (CAPE), or CAPE FALCON, the NW extremity of the island of Sardinia, the ancient Gorditanum. It is in N lat. 41° 1', E long. 8° 9'.
ASINE, a village of Greece, on the gulf of Anapli, to the SE of Anapli.
ASINELLA, a town of Naples, in the Lower Abruzzo, at the mouth of a stream of the same name.
ASIZ (RAS), or ASSEASE, a cape on the E coast of the Red sea, in N lat. 18° 25′, 12½ m. N, 54° W from Ras Shakkul, the coast between forming a deep bay.
AS ITUANOS, a town of Brazil, in the prov. of Seguro, 20 m. SW of Portalegre.

ASK, a town of Persia, in the prov. of Mazanderan, district of Larijan, of which it is the cap. It rises from the banks of the Herhaz river, on a series of natural terraces, on the SE side of the mountain of Demayend, at an alt. of 5,900 ft. above sea-level. It is said to contain 1,000 or 1,500 houses.—Todd.
ASKAN, a town of Nubia, on the r. bank of the Nile, 35 m. N of New Dongola.
ASKATICHE, a lake of Lower Canada, in Saguenay co., which discharges its waters into the Nekoaba, a branch of the St. John.
ASKE, a township in Easby p., Yorkshire, 2½ m. N of Richmond. Area 1,670 acres. Pop. 92.
ASKEATON, a parish and town in co Limerick. The p. has an area of 6,521 acres. Pop. 4,438. The t. is on the river Deel, 2 m. above its confluence with the Shannon, and 5 m. NNW of Rathkeale.
ASKERAN, a fortress of Persia, in Azerdbijan, built on two hills at the entrance of the valley of Shishe.
ASKERMUKRAM, a town of Persia, in Kuzistan, 24 m. S of Tuster. Rice, oranges, mulberries, and the sugar-cane are cultivated in its vicinity.
ASKERNE, a township in the p. of Campsall, Yorkshire, 7 m. N by W of Doncaster. Area 800 acres. Pop. 468.
ASKERSUND, a town of Sweden, in the prov. of Gothland, on the N extremity of Lake Wetter. Pop. in 1833, 871. It has a good harbour on the lake.
ASKERSWELL, a parish in Dorsetshire, 5 m. E of Bridport. Area 2,330 acres. Pop. 233.
ASKERTON, a township in Lanercost p., Cumberland, 13 m. NE of Carlisle, on a branch of the Irthing. Pop. 496.
ASKEVOLD, a town of Norway, in the prov. of N Berghuus, at the entrance of the Dale-fiord.

ASKEW, a township in Bedale p., Yorkshire, 1 m. NE of Bedale. Area 1,660 acres. Pop. 658. ASKHAM, a parish in Nottinghamshire, 3 m. N of Tuxford. Area 1,240 acres. Pop. 288.—Also a p. in Westmoreland, on the Lowther, 5 m. S from Penrith. Area 3,000 acres. Pop. 635.
ASKHAM-BRYAN, or East Askham, a parish in the ainsty of the city of York, 4 m. WSW of York. Area 1,920 acres. Pop. 342.
ASKHAM-RICHARD, or WEST ASKHAM, a parish adjoining the above. Area 960 acres. Pop. 232.
